Design a Ticket / Issue Tracking System (Jira-style)
MEDIUM
role
Senior Software Engineer (SDE-3)
Round 3
Low-Level Design Round
Low-Level Design (LLD)
exp
#812 · Jun 2026
Low-Level Design (LLD)
Object-Oriented Design
State Machine
Strategy
Priority Queue
Concurrency
Design Patterns

▸
View problem statement
Problem statement
Design a Jira-style ticket / issue tracking system: users file tickets, each ticket walks a status lifecycle, tickets are assigned to agents, and a priority backlog feeds triage so the most important unassigned ticket is picked next.

Core Requirements:

File Tickets: create_ticket(title, priority) opens a ticket and places it in the triage backlog.
Status State Machine: a ticket moves OPEN -> IN_PROGRESS -> RESOLVED -> CLOSED, plus REOPENED; illegal jumps are rejected with a typed error.
Assignment: assign a specific ticket to a specific agent; an agent can claim the next highest-priority unassigned ticket; the system can auto-assign via a pluggable policy (round-robin / least-loaded).
Priority Triage: a priority queue (heap) drains the highest-priority unassigned ticket first, oldest-first within a tier.
Reopen: a RESOLVED/CLOSED ticket can be reopened, clearing its owner and returning it to triage.
Concurrency: many agents racing to assign/claim the SAME ticket -> exactly one wins, the rest get a clear error (no double-assignment).

Additional Considerations:

Per-ticket lock for safe state transitions + assignment; lock striping over tickets.
No accepted request is silently dropped; every call reaches a defined outcome.

Design Considerations:

Use Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) principles.
Identify key classes (e.g. TicketService, Ticket, Agent, AssignmentStrategy).
Model the Ticket as a state machine with a legal-transition guard.
Discuss design patterns (State, Strategy, Priority Queue, Template Method, Information Expert).
Focus on extensibility for new assignment policies / lifecycle states and a clean concurrency model.
Let me build and verify the design before presenting it.

input.content:
# Python 3.11+
"""Domain model for the Jira-style Ticket / Issue Tracking System.

The "nouns" of the system live here: the two enums (status + priority), the one
exception family, the `Agent` (an assignee with a live workload), and the star of
the show — `Ticket`, which is a self-guarding STATE MACHINE. Only the ticket is
allowed to change its own status, and it refuses any illegal jump (e.g.
OPEN -> RESOLVED) by construction.

🎙️ "The smart move here is to make the Ticket guard its own lifecycle. The
service never writes `ticket.status = CLOSED` directly — it asks the ticket to
transition, and the ticket rejects anything that isn't a legal step. That makes
a whole class of bugs impossible no matter how the service evolves."
"""

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum, IntEnum


# ── VOCABULARY ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
class Priority(IntEnum):
    # WHY: IntEnum (not plain Enum) so a higher business priority is a higher
    #      number — the triage heap orders by this value with one comparison,
    #      no lookup table needed.
    LOW = 1
    MEDIUM = 2
    HIGH = 3
    CRITICAL = 4


class TicketStatus(Enum):
    OPEN = "OPEN"                # filed, not yet picked up by anyone
    IN_PROGRESS = "IN_PROGRESS"  # an agent owns it and is working on it
    RESOLVED = "RESOLVED"        # fix is in; awaiting verification/close
    CLOSED = "CLOSED"            # done and verified — terminal (until reopened)
    REOPENED = "REOPENED"        # a closed/resolved ticket came back to life


# WHY: the single source of truth for the lifecycle. Every legal step is one
#      edge in this map; anything not listed is rejected. To add a state later
#      you edit ONLY this map — no scattered if/else across the codebase.
_LEGAL_TRANSITIONS: dict[TicketStatus, frozenset[TicketStatus]] = {
    TicketStatus.OPEN: frozenset({TicketStatus.IN_PROGRESS}),
    TicketStatus.IN_PROGRESS: frozenset({TicketStatus.RESOLVED}),
    TicketStatus.RESOLVED: frozenset({TicketStatus.CLOSED, TicketStatus.REOPENED}),
    TicketStatus.CLOSED: frozenset({TicketStatus.REOPENED}),
    TicketStatus.REOPENED: frozenset({TicketStatus.IN_PROGRESS}),
}

# A ticket is up for grabs in triage only in these two states (and unassigned).
_CLAIMABLE_STATES = frozenset({TicketStatus.OPEN, TicketStatus.REOPENED})


# ── EXCEPTIONS (one base, one subclass per failure mode) ─────────────────────
class TicketError(Exception):
    """Base for every error this system raises — callers catch one type."""


class ValidationError(TicketError):
    """A public input was malformed (e.g. an empty ticket title)."""


class TicketNotFoundError(TicketError):
    """Lookup by an unknown ticket id."""


class AgentNotFoundError(TicketError):
    """Assignment to / claim by an unknown agent id."""


class IllegalTransitionError(TicketError):
    """A status change that the state machine forbids (e.g. OPEN -> CLOSED)."""


class AlreadyAssignedError(TicketError):
    """Two parties raced for one ticket; this one lost — it already has an owner."""


class EmptyBacklogError(TicketError):
    """claim_next / auto_assign_next found no claimable ticket in triage."""


class NoAgentAvailableError(TicketError):
    """An assignment strategy was asked to pick from an empty agent pool."""


# ── AGENT: an assignee with a live workload ──────────────────────────────────
@dataclass
class Agent:
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # An agent is more than a name — it carries `active_load`, the count of
    # tickets currently on its plate. The LeastLoaded strategy reads this to
    # spread work fairly, so the load has to be a first-class, live field.
    # 🎙️ "Agent isn't a dumb string. It tracks how many open tickets it owns,
    #     because that number is exactly what a fair-assignment policy needs."
    id: str
    name: str
    active_load: int = 0


# ── TICKET: the lifecycle state machine ──────────────────────────────────────
@dataclass
class Ticket:
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # The heart of the design. A Ticket owns its own status and assignee, and it
    # is the ONLY place those change. `transition` consults the legal-transition
    # map and refuses illegal jumps; `assign` refuses a second owner. Push both
    # rules into the entity and the orchestrator can never corrupt a ticket.
    id: str
    title: str
    priority: Priority
    created_seq: int                       # monotonic at creation; used for FIFO tie-break in triage
    status: TicketStatus = TicketStatus.OPEN
    assignee_id: str | None = None

    def is_claimable(self) -> bool:
        # WHY: "up for grabs" means nobody owns it AND it's in an open-ish state.
        #      Both conditions matter — a RESOLVED ticket still has an assignee,
        #      so it must never look claimable.
        return self.assignee_id is None and self.status in _CLAIMABLE_STATES

    def assign(self, agent_id: str) -> None:
        # WHY: the no-double-assignment rule lives HERE, on the ticket, so it
        #      holds no matter which service path (manual assign, claim, auto)
        #      tries to set an owner. Whoever checks-and-sets first wins; the
        #      loser sees assignee_id is already set and is rejected.
        if self.assignee_id is not None:
            raise AlreadyAssignedError(
                f"{self.id} is already assigned to {self.assignee_id}"
            )
        self.assignee_id = agent_id

    def transition(self, new_status: TicketStatus) -> None:
        # WHY: one guard, one truth. If the step isn't an edge in the legal map,
        #      it's rejected — so OPEN -> RESOLVED (skipping work) is impossible.
        if new_status not in _LEGAL_TRANSITIONS[self.status]:
            raise IllegalTransitionError(
                f"{self.id}: cannot move {self.status.value} -> {new_status.value}"
            )
        self.status = new_status

    def reopen(self) -> None:
        # WHY: reopening drops the old owner and sends the ticket back to triage.
        #      We go through `transition` so the legality check still applies —
        #      only RESOLVED or CLOSED tickets may reopen; anything else is
        #      rejected with the same clear error as any other illegal jump.
        self.transition(TicketStatus.REOPENED)
        self.assignee_id = None

# Python 3.11+
"""Assignment policies — the seam that decides WHICH agent a system-triaged
ticket goes to.

The service never hardcodes "give it to the next agent" or "give it to the
quietest agent". It asks an `AssignmentStrategy`. Swapping the policy (round
robin today, least-loaded tomorrow, skill-based next quarter) is a new class
with zero changes to the orchestrator.

🎙️ "This is the Strategy pattern. The ticket service depends on the abstract
`AssignmentStrategy`, not on any concrete rule — so the rule for picking an
assignee is pluggable. New policy = new class, core untouched."
"""

from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
from itertools import count

from models import Agent, NoAgentAvailableError, Ticket


class AssignmentStrategy(ABC):
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # The abstraction the orchestrator depends on (Dependency Inversion). Given
    # the live agent pool and the ticket being placed, return the agent who
    # should own it. Concretes differ only in HOW they choose.
    @abstractmethod
    def pick(self, agents: list[Agent], ticket: Ticket) -> Agent:
        """Choose an assignee for `ticket` from `agents`, or raise if none exist."""
        raise NotImplementedError


class RoundRobin(AssignmentStrategy):
    """Fair-by-rotation: hand tickets to agents in a repeating cycle."""

    def __init__(self) -> None:
        # WHY: an endless counter; we mod by the live agent count at pick time so
        #      the rotation self-adjusts if the pool size changes between calls.
        self._cursor = count()

    def pick(self, agents: list[Agent], ticket: Ticket) -> Agent:
        if not agents:
            raise NoAgentAvailableError("no agents registered to take tickets")
        return agents[next(self._cursor) % len(agents)]


class LeastLoaded(AssignmentStrategy):
    """Fair-by-load: give the next ticket to whoever has the fewest open ones."""

    def pick(self, agents: list[Agent], ticket: Ticket) -> Agent:
        if not agents:
            raise NoAgentAvailableError("no agents registered to take tickets")
        # WHY: min by live workload spreads work toward idle agents. Ties resolve
        #      to the first agent in the list, which keeps the choice deterministic
        #      and easy to assert in tests.
        return min(agents, key=lambda a: a.active_load)

# Python 3.11+
"""TicketService — the orchestrator — plus its thread-safe subclass.

`TicketService` is the clean, single-threaded-correct core: it owns the ticket
registry, the agent pool, the pluggable assignment strategy, and the priority
backlog (a heap) used for triage. It contains ZERO lock code — concurrency is a
second, deliberate layer.

`ThreadSafeTicketService` is that second layer: it overrides two no-op hooks
(`_lock` for per-ticket striping, `_backlog_guard` for the heap) with real locks.
The core never changes; the diff is ~12 lines. That's the Template-Method seam.

🎙️ "I keep the core lock-free so the object design is the star, then add a
thread-safe subclass that overrides one per-ticket-lock hook and one backlog
hook. Clean version and locked version sit side by side; the diff is tiny."
"""

import heapq
import threading
from collections.abc import Iterator
from contextlib import contextmanager

from models import (
    Agent,
    AlreadyAssignedError,
    AgentNotFoundError,
    EmptyBacklogError,
    Priority,
    Ticket,
    TicketNotFoundError,
    TicketStatus,
    ValidationError,
)
from strategies import AssignmentStrategy, RoundRobin


class TicketService:
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # The single front door. Every actor — reporter, agent, triage bot — calls
    # this. It owns the truth: the registry of all tickets, the agent pool, and
    # the triage backlog. It coordinates but delegates the rules: the Ticket
    # guards its own status, the Strategy picks the assignee.
    def __init__(
        self,
        agents: list[Agent] | None = None,
        strategy: AssignmentStrategy | None = None,
    ) -> None:
        self._tickets: dict[str, Ticket] = {}
        self._agents: dict[str, Agent] = {a.id: a for a in (agents or [])}
        # WHY: default to RoundRobin so the service is usable out of the box;
        #      inject LeastLoaded (or any future policy) without touching code.
        self._strategy: AssignmentStrategy = strategy or RoundRobin()
        # WHY: a binary heap keyed by (-priority, created_seq) so the highest
        #      priority (and, within a tier, the oldest) always pops first.
        self._backlog: list[tuple[int, int, str]] = []
        self._seq = 0  # monotonic; doubles as the ticket id suffix and FIFO tie-break

    # ── concurrency hooks (no-ops here; the thread-safe subclass overrides) ──
    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self, ticket: Ticket) -> Iterator[None]:
        # WHY: does nothing in the clean core — no lock code lives in this file.
        #      The thread-safe subclass replaces this with a real per-ticket lock.
        yield

    @contextmanager
    def _backlog_guard(self) -> Iterator[None]:
        # WHY: guards the shared heap + registry. No-op here; real lock in the subclass.
        yield

    # ── create ──
    def create_ticket(self, title: str, priority: Priority) -> Ticket:
        # WHY: validate at the boundary BEFORE mutating anything — an empty title
        #      is rejected with a typed error, never stored.
        if not title or not title.strip():
            raise ValidationError("ticket title must be a non-empty string")
        with self._backlog_guard():
            self._seq += 1
            ticket = Ticket(
                id=f"tkt-{self._seq}",
                title=title.strip(),
                priority=priority,
                created_seq=self._seq,
            )
            self._tickets[ticket.id] = ticket
            self._enqueue(ticket)
        return ticket

    # ── assign (manual, the contended path) ──
    def assign_ticket(self, ticket_id: str, user_id: str) -> Ticket:
        # WHY: this is the race-prone method — many parties may grab one ticket.
        #      The check-and-set lives inside `ticket.assign`, under the per-ticket
        #      lock, so exactly one caller wins and the rest get AlreadyAssignedError.
        ticket = self._require_ticket(ticket_id)
        agent = self._require_agent(user_id)
        with self._lock(ticket):
            ticket.assign(user_id)                              # raises if already owned
            # WHY: start work via the public transition — it re-acquires the SAME
            #      per-ticket lock, which is exactly why the lock must be reentrant.
            self.transition(ticket_id, TicketStatus.IN_PROGRESS)
            self._apply_load_delta(agent, +1)
        return ticket

    # ── triage: agent self-serve (priority-queue drain) ──
    def claim_next(self, user_id: str) -> Ticket:
        # WHY: an agent asks for "the most important thing waiting". We pop the
        #      top of the heap, then assign — reusing assign_ticket so the
        #      ownership guard is identical. A lost race just pops the next one.
        self._require_agent(user_id)
        while True:
            with self._backlog_guard():
                ticket = self._pop_claimable()                 # raises EmptyBacklogError
            try:
                return self.assign_ticket(ticket.id, user_id)
            except AlreadyAssignedError:
                continue                                       # someone beat us; try the next

    # ── triage: system-driven via Strategy ──
    def auto_assign_next(self) -> Ticket:
        # WHY: the system places the top ticket onto an agent chosen by the
        #      pluggable Strategy (round-robin / least-loaded). Strategy.pick runs
        #      under the backlog lock so a stateful policy (round-robin cursor) is safe.
        while True:
            with self._backlog_guard():
                ticket = self._pop_claimable()
                agent = self._strategy.pick(list(self._agents.values()), ticket)
            try:
                return self.assign_ticket(ticket.id, agent.id)
            except AlreadyAssignedError:
                continue

    # ── status changes ──
    def transition(self, ticket_id: str, status: TicketStatus) -> Ticket:
        # WHY: the generic guarded state move. The Ticket itself rejects illegal
        #      jumps; here we only add the per-ticket lock and the load bookkeeping
        #      when work is resolved (the agent's plate clears).
        ticket = self._require_ticket(ticket_id)
        with self._lock(ticket):
            ticket.transition(status)
            if status is TicketStatus.RESOLVED and ticket.assignee_id is not None:
                self._apply_load_delta(self._agents[ticket.assignee_id], -1)
        return ticket

    def reopen(self, ticket_id: str) -> Ticket:
        # WHY: reopening drops the old owner (in `ticket.reopen`) and pushes the
        #      ticket back onto triage so it can be claimed again — two short,
        #      separate critical sections, never nested, so no deadlock.
        ticket = self._require_ticket(ticket_id)
        with self._lock(ticket):
            ticket.reopen()
        with self._backlog_guard():
            self._enqueue(ticket)
        return ticket

    # ── read ──
    def list_backlog(self) -> list[Ticket]:
        # WHY: a point-in-time snapshot of what's claimable, in the exact order
        #      triage would drain it: highest priority first, oldest first within
        #      a tier. Computed from the registry so it never shows stale heap junk.
        claimable = [t for t in self._tickets.values() if t.is_claimable()]
        return sorted(claimable, key=lambda t: (-t.priority.value, t.created_seq))

    # ── private helpers ──
    def _enqueue(self, ticket: Ticket) -> None:
        # Caller MUST hold the backlog guard. Negate priority so a max-priority
        # ticket sorts smallest in Python's min-heap; created_seq breaks ties FIFO.
        heapq.heappush(
            self._backlog, (-ticket.priority.value, ticket.created_seq, ticket.id)
        )

    def _pop_claimable(self) -> Ticket:
        # Caller MUST hold the backlog guard. Lazy deletion: a ticket grabbed via
        # assign_ticket stays in the heap as a stale entry; we skip any that are no
        # longer claimable until we find a live one (or the heap runs dry).
        while self._backlog:
            _, _, ticket_id = heapq.heappop(self._backlog)
            ticket = self._tickets[ticket_id]
            if ticket.is_claimable():
                return ticket
        raise EmptyBacklogError("no claimable tickets in the triage backlog")

    def _apply_load_delta(self, agent: Agent, delta: int) -> None:
        agent.active_load += delta

    def _require_ticket(self, ticket_id: str) -> Ticket:
        ticket = self._tickets.get(ticket_id)
        if ticket is None:
            raise TicketNotFoundError(f"no ticket with id {ticket_id!r}")
        return ticket

    def _require_agent(self, user_id: str) -> Agent:
        agent = self._agents.get(user_id)
        if agent is None:
            raise AgentNotFoundError(f"no agent with id {user_id!r}")
        return agent


class ThreadSafeTicketService(TicketService):
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # The ONLY place locks exist. It overrides the two no-op hooks: `_lock` gives
    # each ticket its own reentrant lock (lock striping — two agents on two
    # different tickets never block each other), and `_backlog_guard` serializes
    # the shared heap. The core methods above are inherited UNCHANGED.
    def __init__(
        self,
        agents: list[Agent] | None = None,
        strategy: AssignmentStrategy | None = None,
    ) -> None:
        super().__init__(agents, strategy)
        self._locks: dict[str, threading.RLock] = {}
        self._map_lock = threading.Lock()        # guards the dict-of-locks itself
        self._backlog_lock = threading.Lock()    # guards the heap + registry writes

    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self, ticket: Ticket) -> Iterator[None]:
        # WHY: lock the map first to fetch-or-create this ticket's lock (two
        #      threads adding a new key at once could corrupt the dict), then hold
        #      the per-ticket lock. RLock so the same thread can re-enter — e.g.
        #      assign_ticket holds it and calls transition, which re-takes it.
        with self._map_lock:
            lock = self._locks.setdefault(ticket.id, threading.RLock())
        with lock:
            yield

    @contextmanager
    def _backlog_guard(self) -> Iterator[None]:
        with self._backlog_lock:
            yield

# Python 3.11+
"""Narrated demo driver for the Jira-style Ticket / Issue Tracking System.

Run: `python3 main.py`. Each case prints a one-line "Case N ... -> OK" so a
green run is obvious at a glance. Rigorous coverage lives in tests/.
"""

import threading

from models import (
    Agent,
    AlreadyAssignedError,
    IllegalTransitionError,
    Priority,
    TicketStatus,
)
from strategies import LeastLoaded
from ticket_service import ThreadSafeTicketService, TicketService


def main() -> None:
    # ── Case 1: triage orders the backlog by priority, oldest-first on ties ──
    svc = TicketService(agents=[Agent("u1", "Ana"), Agent("u2", "Ben")])
    low = svc.create_ticket("typo in footer", Priority.LOW)
    crit = svc.create_ticket("checkout is down", Priority.CRITICAL)
    high = svc.create_ticket("login slow", Priority.HIGH)
    med = svc.create_ticket("export missing column", Priority.MEDIUM)
    order = [t.id for t in svc.list_backlog()]
    print(f"Case 1: backlog drains in order {order}")
    assert order == [crit.id, high.id, med.id, low.id], order
    print("Case 1: highest priority sits at the front of triage -> OK")

    # ── Case 2: claim_next hands the top ticket to the agent who asks ──
    first = svc.claim_next("u1")
    second = svc.claim_next("u2")
    print(f"Case 2: u1 claimed {first.id} ({first.priority.name}), u2 claimed {second.id} ({second.priority.name})")
    assert first.id == crit.id and first.status is TicketStatus.IN_PROGRESS
    assert second.id == high.id and second.assignee_id == "u2"
    print("Case 2: agents drain the queue most-important-first -> OK")

    # ── Case 3: a ticket walks its full legal lifecycle ──
    svc.transition(crit.id, TicketStatus.RESOLVED)
    svc.transition(crit.id, TicketStatus.CLOSED)
    print(f"Case 3: {crit.id} lifecycle -> {crit.status.name}")
    assert crit.status is TicketStatus.CLOSED
    print("Case 3: OPEN -> IN_PROGRESS -> RESOLVED -> CLOSED all legal -> OK")

    # ── Case 4: an illegal jump is rejected by the state machine ──
    try:
        svc.transition(med.id, TicketStatus.CLOSED)   # med is still OPEN
        assert False, "should have rejected OPEN -> CLOSED"
    except IllegalTransitionError as e:
        print(f"Case 4: rejected illegal jump -> {e}")
    print("Case 4: OPEN -> CLOSED (skipping work) is impossible by construction -> OK")

    # ── Case 5: reopening a closed ticket sends it back into triage ──
    svc.reopen(crit.id)
    back = [t.id for t in svc.list_backlog()]
    print(f"Case 5: after reopen, {crit.id} is claimable again; backlog now {back}")
    assert crit.id in back and crit.status is TicketStatus.REOPENED
    assert crit.assignee_id is None
    print("Case 5: reopen clears the owner and re-queues the ticket -> OK")

    # ── Case 6: many agents race for ONE ticket; exactly one wins ──
    n = 32
    pool = [Agent(f"a{i}", f"Agent{i}") for i in range(n)]
    racy = ThreadSafeTicketService(agents=pool)
    hot = racy.create_ticket("prod outage", Priority.CRITICAL)
    wins: list[str] = []
    losses: list[Exception] = []
    lock = threading.Lock()
    barrier = threading.Barrier(n)

    def grab(agent_id: str) -> None:
        barrier.wait()                        # all n hit assign at the same instant
        try:
            t = racy.assign_ticket(hot.id, agent_id)
            with lock:
                wins.append(t.assignee_id)
        except AlreadyAssignedError as e:
            with lock:
                losses.append(e)

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=grab, args=(f"a{i}",)) for i in range(n)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()
    print(f"Case 6: {n} agents raced for {hot.id} -> {len(wins)} won, {len(losses)} rejected")
    assert len(wins) == 1, wins
    assert len(losses) == n - 1
    assert hot.assignee_id == wins[0] and hot.status is TicketStatus.IN_PROGRESS
    print("Case 6: exactly ONE assignee wins, the rest get AlreadyAssignedError -> OK")

    # ── Case 7: system auto-assigns by least-loaded policy ──
    auto = TicketService(agents=[Agent("x", "Xena"), Agent("y", "Yann")], strategy=LeastLoaded())
    for i in range(4):
        auto.create_ticket(f"bug {i}", Priority.MEDIUM)
    placed = [auto.auto_assign_next().assignee_id for _ in range(4)]
    loads = {a.id: a.active_load for a in auto._agents.values()}
    print(f"Case 7: least-loaded spread 4 tickets as loads {loads}")
    assert loads == {"x": 2, "y": 2}, loads
    print("Case 7: LeastLoaded balances work evenly across agents -> OK")

    print("\nAll cases passed.")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

import pathlib
import sys

# Put the workspace root on sys.path so `from models import ...` works in tests.
sys.path.insert(0, str(pathlib.Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent))

# Python 3.11+
"""pytest suite for the Jira-style Ticket / Issue Tracking System.

Covers: triage priority ordering, the full legal lifecycle + every illegal jump,
manual assignment + the typed error paths, reopen re-queuing, both assignment
strategies, and TWO concurrency proofs driven by threading.Barrier so every
thread hits the critical section at one instant:
  1. N agents race for ONE ticket -> exactly one wins (no double-assignment).
  2. M agents claim from an M-ticket backlog -> M distinct tickets, no dupes.
"""

import threading

import pytest

from models import (
    Agent,
    AgentNotFoundError,
    AlreadyAssignedError,
    EmptyBacklogError,
    IllegalTransitionError,
    NoAgentAvailableError,
    Priority,
    TicketNotFoundError,
    TicketStatus,
    ValidationError,
)
from strategies import LeastLoaded, RoundRobin
from ticket_service import ThreadSafeTicketService, TicketService


# ── create + validation ──
def test_create_ticket_opens_unassigned():
    svc = TicketService()
    t = svc.create_ticket("login broken", Priority.HIGH)
    assert t.id == "tkt-1"
    assert t.status is TicketStatus.OPEN
    assert t.assignee_id is None
    assert t.is_claimable()


@pytest.mark.parametrize("bad", ["", "   "])
def test_create_ticket_empty_title_rejected(bad):
    svc = TicketService()
    with pytest.raises(ValidationError):
        svc.create_ticket(bad, Priority.LOW)


# ── triage / priority queue ──
def test_backlog_orders_by_priority_then_fifo():
    svc = TicketService()
    a = svc.create_ticket("a", Priority.LOW)
    b = svc.create_ticket("b", Priority.CRITICAL)
    c = svc.create_ticket("c", Priority.CRITICAL)   # same tier, later -> after b
    d = svc.create_ticket("d", Priority.HIGH)
    assert [t.id for t in svc.list_backlog()] == [b.id, c.id, d.id, a.id]


def test_claim_next_drains_highest_priority_first():
    svc = TicketService(agents=[Agent("u", "U")])
    lo = svc.create_ticket("lo", Priority.LOW)
    hi = svc.create_ticket("hi", Priority.HIGH)
    assert svc.claim_next("u").id == hi.id
    assert svc.claim_next("u").id == lo.id
    with pytest.raises(EmptyBacklogError):
        svc.claim_next("u")


def test_list_backlog_excludes_assigned():
    svc = TicketService(agents=[Agent("u", "U")])
    a = svc.create_ticket("a", Priority.LOW)
    b = svc.create_ticket("b", Priority.HIGH)
    svc.assign_ticket(b.id, "u")
    assert [t.id for t in svc.list_backlog()] == [a.id]


def test_claim_next_empty_backlog_raises():
    svc = TicketService(agents=[Agent("u", "U")])
    with pytest.raises(EmptyBacklogError):
        svc.claim_next("u")


def test_claim_next_unknown_agent_rejected():
    svc = TicketService()
    with pytest.raises(AgentNotFoundError):
        svc.claim_next("ghost")


# ── manual assignment + typed errors ──
def test_assign_ticket_sets_owner_and_starts_work():
    svc = TicketService(agents=[Agent("u", "U")])
    t = svc.create_ticket("x", Priority.LOW)
    out = svc.assign_ticket(t.id, "u")
    assert out.assignee_id == "u"
    assert out.status is TicketStatus.IN_PROGRESS
    assert svc._agents["u"].active_load == 1


def test_assign_already_assigned_rejected():
    svc = TicketService(agents=[Agent("u", "U"), Agent("v", "V")])
    t = svc.create_ticket("x", Priority.LOW)
    svc.assign_ticket(t.id, "u")
    with pytest.raises(AlreadyAssignedError):
        svc.assign_ticket(t.id, "v")


def test_assign_unknown_ticket_rejected():
    svc = TicketService(agents=[Agent("u", "U")])
    with pytest.raises(TicketNotFoundError):
        svc.assign_ticket("nope", "u")


def test_assign_unknown_agent_rejected():
    svc = TicketService(agents=[Agent("u", "U")])
    t = svc.create_ticket("x", Priority.LOW)
    with pytest.raises(AgentNotFoundError):
        svc.assign_ticket(t.id, "ghost")


# ── state machine: legal + illegal ──
def test_full_legal_lifecycle():
    svc = TicketService(agents=[Agent("u", "U")])
    t = svc.create_ticket("x", Priority.LOW)
    svc.assign_ticket(t.id, "u")                       # OPEN -> IN_PROGRESS
    svc.transition(t.id, TicketStatus.RESOLVED)        # IN_PROGRESS -> RESOLVED
    svc.transition(t.id, TicketStatus.CLOSED)          # RESOLVED -> CLOSED
    assert t.status is TicketStatus.CLOSED


@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    "bad", [TicketStatus.RESOLVED, TicketStatus.CLOSED, TicketStatus.REOPENED, TicketStatus.OPEN]
)
def test_illegal_transition_from_open_rejected(bad):
    svc = TicketService()
    t = svc.create_ticket("x", Priority.LOW)
    with pytest.raises(IllegalTransitionError):
        svc.transition(t.id, bad)


def test_transition_unknown_ticket_rejected():
    svc = TicketService()
    with pytest.raises(TicketNotFoundError):
        svc.transition("nope", TicketStatus.IN_PROGRESS)


def test_resolve_frees_agent_load():
    svc = TicketService(agents=[Agent("u", "U")])
    t = svc.create_ticket("x", Priority.LOW)
    svc.assign_ticket(t.id, "u")
    assert svc._agents["u"].active_load == 1
    svc.transition(t.id, TicketStatus.RESOLVED)
    assert svc._agents["u"].active_load == 0


# ── reopen ──
def test_reopen_requeues_and_clears_owner():
    svc = TicketService(agents=[Agent("u", "U")])
    t = svc.create_ticket("x", Priority.HIGH)
    svc.assign_ticket(t.id, "u")
    svc.transition(t.id, TicketStatus.RESOLVED)
    svc.transition(t.id, TicketStatus.CLOSED)
    svc.reopen(t.id)
    assert t.status is TicketStatus.REOPENED
    assert t.assignee_id is None
    assert t.id in [x.id for x in svc.list_backlog()]
    again = svc.claim_next("u")                        # claimable once more
    assert again.id == t.id
    assert again.status is TicketStatus.IN_PROGRESS


def test_reopen_open_ticket_rejected():
    svc = TicketService()
    t = svc.create_ticket("x", Priority.LOW)
    with pytest.raises(IllegalTransitionError):
        svc.reopen(t.id)


# ── assignment strategies ──
def test_auto_assign_round_robin_cycles():
    svc = TicketService(agents=[Agent("u", "U"), Agent("v", "V")], strategy=RoundRobin())
    for i in range(4):
        svc.create_ticket(f"t{i}", Priority.MEDIUM)
    owners = [svc.auto_assign_next().assignee_id for _ in range(4)]
    assert owners == ["u", "v", "u", "v"]


def test_auto_assign_least_loaded_balances():
    svc = TicketService(agents=[Agent("u", "U"), Agent("v", "V")], strategy=LeastLoaded())
    for i in range(4):
        svc.create_ticket(f"t{i}", Priority.MEDIUM)
    for _ in range(4):
        svc.auto_assign_next()
    assert svc._agents["u"].active_load == 2
    assert svc._agents["v"].active_load == 2


def test_auto_assign_empty_pool_raises():
    svc = TicketService(agents=[], strategy=RoundRobin())
    svc.create_ticket("x", Priority.LOW)
    with pytest.raises(NoAgentAvailableError):
        svc.auto_assign_next()


# ── concurrency proof 1: N agents race for ONE ticket, exactly one wins ──
def test_concurrent_assign_exactly_one_winner():
    n = 50
    pool = [Agent(f"a{i}", f"A{i}") for i in range(n)]
    svc = ThreadSafeTicketService(agents=pool)
    hot = svc.create_ticket("prod outage", Priority.CRITICAL)

    wins: list[str] = []
    losses: list[Exception] = []
    guard = threading.Lock()
    barrier = threading.Barrier(n)

    def attempt(i: int) -> None:
        barrier.wait()                                 # all n hit assign at one instant
        try:
            t = svc.assign_ticket(hot.id, f"a{i}")
            with guard:
                wins.append(t.assignee_id)
        except AlreadyAssignedError as e:
            with guard:
                losses.append(e)

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=attempt, args=(i,)) for i in range(n)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()

    assert len(wins) == 1                              # exactly one success
    assert len(set(wins)) == 1                         # the winner is a single agent
    assert hot.assignee_id == wins[0]                  # entity agrees with the winner
    assert hot.status is TicketStatus.IN_PROGRESS      # work started exactly once
    assert len(losses) == n - 1                        # everyone else lost
    assert all(isinstance(e, AlreadyAssignedError) for e in losses)
    assert len([a for a in pool if a.active_load == 1]) == 1   # one plate grew, by one


# ── concurrency proof 2: M agents drain an M-ticket backlog, no duplicates ──
def test_concurrent_claim_no_ticket_claimed_twice():
    m = 30
    pool = [Agent(f"a{i}", f"A{i}") for i in range(m)]
    svc = ThreadSafeTicketService(agents=pool)
    for i in range(m):
        svc.create_ticket(f"t{i}", Priority.MEDIUM)

    claimed: list[str] = []
    guard = threading.Lock()
    barrier = threading.Barrier(m)

    def attempt(i: int) -> None:
        barrier.wait()
        ticket = svc.claim_next(f"a{i}")
        with guard:
            claimed.append(ticket.id)

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=attempt, args=(i,)) for i in range(m)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()

    assert len(claimed) == m                           # every agent got one
    assert len(set(claimed)) == m                      # and no ticket went to two agents
    assert svc.list_backlog() == []                    # backlog fully drained

content.text:
All tests pass. Here's the complete, interview-ready design.

> 🎙️ **Open with this framing** (it's the senior move for this problem): "A ticket tracker looks like CRUD, but the real meat is three things that interact: a ticket's *status lifecycle* must never make an illegal jump, *assignment* must never give one ticket two owners, and *triage* has to always pull the most important thing next. So I'll make the **Ticket a self-guarding state machine**, make the **assignment policy a Strategy**, and run triage off a **priority heap** — then the only hard part left is making the single-owner check atomic under concurrency."

## ⏱️ 60-Minute Interview Plan

| Time | What you do | Mode |
|------|-------------|------|
| 0–5 min | **Say §1–2 out loud** — restate "Jira-style tracker", lock scope (one project, in-memory, no auth/notifications). | 🗣️ SAY |
| 5–8 min | **Pin the API surface (§3.5)** — 7 public methods on `TicketService` (create / assign / claim_next / auto_assign_next / transition / reopen / list_backlog). | 🗣️ SAY |
| 8–14 min | **Draw the core class diagram** — `TicketService`, `Ticket` (state machine), `AssignmentStrategy` + 2 concretes, `Agent`. Mention `Priority`/`TicketStatus` enums verbally. | ✍️ DRAW |
| 14–16 min | **State patterns + concurrency flag** — State + Strategy + priority heap; then: "I see a check-then-act race in `assign_ticket` — two agents grab one ticket; I'll fix it with a per-ticket lock in §9." | 🗣️ SAY |
| 16–18 min | **Walk §5.5 approach** — the key insight (entity guards both rules) + the heap ordering trick (`-priority, seq`). | 🗣️ SAY |
| 18–38 min | **Write code in interview order** (§6): enums → `Ticket` state machine → `AssignmentStrategy` → `TicketService` skeleton → `assign_ticket` → `claim_next`. | ✍️ WRITE |
| 38–45 min | **Demo Case 1 + Case 6** — triage order + the contended-assign race. Run `main.py` if it's a coding env. | 🗣️ SAY / run |
| 45–55 min | **Concurrency answer** — per-ticket `RLock`, lock striping, why reentrant. Write `ThreadSafeTicketService` only if asked. | 🗣️ SAY (❓ code if asked) |
| 55–60 min | **Follow-ups** — distributed assignment, 10M tickets, reopened-SLA twist. | ❓ IF ASKED |

**Two interview modes — pick one at the top:**
- **Mode A — Whiteboard / no-run (Amazon, Google):** Skip §7 run output. Demo cases verbally. Write code but do not execute.
- **Mode B — Coding environment (Uber, Meta, startups):** Write and RUN `main.py` in §7. Show actual terminal output.

**The one thing that makes interviewers flag you "strong hire":** right after the diagram, say *"there's a check-then-act race in `assign_ticket`"* before they ask. That single proactive sentence signals you've built real concurrent systems.

---

## 1. Understanding the Problem & Clarifying Questions

### 1.1 What we're building (plain words)
We're building the engine behind a Jira-style issue tracker. People file tickets (bugs, tasks), each ticket walks a lifecycle — it starts **OPEN**, someone picks it up so it's **IN_PROGRESS**, then it gets **RESOLVED**, then **CLOSED**; and a closed thing can come back as **REOPENED**. The interesting parts aren't the CRUD — they're that the lifecycle has *legal* steps only, that a ticket can have at most *one* owner even when several agents grab it at once, and that triage always pulls the *highest-priority* unassigned ticket next.

### 1.2 Clarifying questions (candidate-led proposals)

**You:** "I'll scope this to a single project's ticket store, in-memory, one process for the core design — and treat auth, notifications, and the web layer as out of scope since they're separate systems. That OK?"
**Interviewer:** "Yep, focus on the domain model and concurrency."
*✓ scoped: in-memory orchestrator is the unit under design; I'll flag distributed in §9.*

**You:** "For the lifecycle I'll use OPEN → IN_PROGRESS → RESOLVED → CLOSED, plus REOPENED for a ticket that comes back. Illegal jumps like OPEN → CLOSED get rejected with a typed error. Sound right?"
**Interviewer:** "That's exactly the model."
*✓ the legal-transition map is the single source of truth.*

**You:** "Two ways a ticket gets an owner: an agent or lead **assigns** a specific ticket to a specific person, and an agent **claims** the next most-important thing off the backlog. I'll also add a system **auto-assign** that uses a policy to pick the agent. Is that the full assignment surface?"
**Interviewer:** "Good — and make the auto-assign policy swappable."
*✓ that's the Strategy seam: RoundRobin and LeastLoaded for v1.*

**You:** "Triage priority: I'll do four tiers LOW/MEDIUM/HIGH/CRITICAL, highest drains first, and within a tier it's oldest-first so nothing starves. Fine?"
**Interviewer:** "Fine. FIFO within a tier is the right call."
*✓ heap key is (-priority, creation_seq).*

**You:** "The edge I most care about: if two agents try to grab the *same* ticket at the same instant, exactly one wins and the other gets a clear 'already assigned' error — never two owners. I'll make the ticket itself enforce single-ownership. Agreed that's the key invariant?"
**Interviewer:** "Yes — that's the thing I'd push on. Show me it's atomic."
*✓ this is the §9 proof: a 50-thread Barrier test.*

**You:** "Reopening: I'll have it drop the old owner and push the ticket back into triage so it gets re-claimed. Reasonable, or should it keep the original assignee?"
**Interviewer:** "Re-queue it — reopened work should go through triage again."
*✓ reopen clears assignee + re-enqueues.*

### 1.3 Final Requirements (the whiteboard summary)

**Functional Requirements**
1. **File a ticket** with a title and priority.
   - Returns a ticket in `OPEN`, unassigned; rejects an empty title with a typed error.
2. **Triage backlog** of unassigned tickets, ordered highest-priority-first, oldest-first within a tier.
   - `list_backlog()` returns the snapshot; `claim_next(agent)` pops the top one for that agent.
3. **Assign a specific ticket to a specific agent.**
   - Sets the owner and starts work (→ IN_PROGRESS); rejects a second owner with `AlreadyAssignedError`.
4. **Auto-assign** the top ticket to an agent chosen by a pluggable policy (RoundRobin / LeastLoaded).
5. **Walk the status lifecycle** OPEN → IN_PROGRESS → RESOLVED → CLOSED, with illegal jumps rejected.
6. **Reopen** a RESOLVED/CLOSED ticket → REOPENED, owner cleared, back into triage.
7. **Concurrency safety:** many agents racing for one ticket → exactly one wins; draining the backlog concurrently → no ticket goes to two agents.

**Out of scope**
- Auth / permissions (who may assign whom) — separate identity system.
- Notifications, email, web/REST layer — downstream; we just expose method calls.
- Persistence / multi-process — in-memory core; distributed atomicity discussed in §9.
- Comments, attachments, labels, sprints — not load-bearing for the lifecycle/triage story.

> 💬 **What you say to open:** "Before modeling, the one thing I want to nail is the single-owner invariant — a ticket never gets two assignees even under a race. With that locked, let me walk the actors and flows."

---

## 2. Actors & Core Flows

**Actors**
- **Reporter** — files a ticket (title + priority). Doesn't own state in our model.
- **Agent** — claims/gets assigned tickets and moves them through the lifecycle. Carries a live workload.
- **Triage lead / system** — runs auto-assign to place backlog tickets onto agents by policy.

**Core flows**
1. Reporter: `create_ticket(title, priority)` → ticket OPEN, lands in the priority backlog.
2. Agent: `claim_next(agent)` → top-priority unassigned ticket → owned + IN_PROGRESS.
3. Lead: `assign_ticket(ticket, agent)` → specific ticket → that agent, IN_PROGRESS.
4. Agent: `transition(ticket, RESOLVED)` → `transition(ticket, CLOSED)` → lifecycle complete.
5. Anyone: `reopen(ticket)` → REOPENED, owner cleared, back in triage for re-claim.

---

## 3. Core Entities (with explicit accept / reject reasoning)

### 3.1 Candidate noun walkthrough (accept / reject one by one)

> **Ticket** — A real thing with identity, a status that changes over time, an owner, and rules about how it may change. Owns both data AND behavior (it guards its own transitions and single-ownership). *Accepted — it's the star entity.*
>
> **Agent** — The person work is assigned to. More than a name: it carries `active_load`, the count of open tickets it owns, which a fair-assignment policy reads. State + identity. *Accepted.*
>
> **AssignmentStrategy** — Not a noun from the prompt, but the *rule* for picking an agent is exactly what the business will tweak. Modeling it as a first-class interface is what makes the policy swappable. *Accepted — as an interface with RoundRobin/LeastLoaded concretes.*
>
> **TicketService (orchestrator)** — The coordinator that holds all tickets, the agent pool, the backlog, and the policy. It's where the public API lives. *Accepted — the brain.*
>
> **Priority** — A finite, *ordered* set of values (LOW…CRITICAL) with no behavior. *Rejected as entity — it's an `IntEnum` (ordering matters for the heap).*
>
> **Status / State** — A finite set of lifecycle states. Tempting to make each a class (State pattern as classes), but here the transitions are a small fixed map — an enum + a transition table is clearer than five state classes. *Rejected as entity — `TicketStatus` enum + a legal-transition map.*
>
> **Backlog / Queue** — Feels like an entity, but it's a *data structure* the orchestrator owns (a heap), not a domain object with its own identity. *Rejected — it's a field (`_backlog`) on the service.*
>
> **Reporter / Triage lead** — Actors that *call* the system; we don't store them. *Rejected — actors live outside the model.*
>
> **Comment / Label / Sprint** — Real Jira nouns, but none affect the lifecycle/triage/assignment story. *Rejected — out of scope; they'd be plain data hung off Ticket later.*

### 3.2 Final entity table (accepted entities only)

| Entity | Responsibility | Key fields (typed) | Invariant it guards |
|---|---|---|---|
| `TicketService` | Coordinate everything: registry, agents, backlog, policy | `_tickets: dict[str, Ticket]` (id), `_agents: dict[str, Agent]`, `_strategy: AssignmentStrategy` (policy seam), `_backlog: list[tuple]` (heap) | Every accepted call reaches a defined outcome; backlog pops highest-priority-first |
| `Ticket` | Be a self-guarding lifecycle state machine with at most one owner | `id: str` (id), `title: str`, `priority: Priority`, `created_seq: int`, `status: TicketStatus`, `assignee_id: str \| None` | Only legal status jumps; never a second assignee |
| `Agent` | Represent an assignee and its live workload | `id: str` (id), `name: str`, `active_load: int` | `active_load` reflects currently-owned open tickets |
| `AssignmentStrategy` | Pick which agent gets an auto-assigned ticket | (interface) | A non-empty pool yields exactly one chosen agent |

**Enums**
- `Priority(IntEnum)`: `LOW=1`, `MEDIUM=2`, `HIGH=3`, `CRITICAL=4` — ordering matters, so IntEnum.
- `TicketStatus(Enum)`: `OPEN`, `IN_PROGRESS`, `RESOLVED`, `CLOSED`, `REOPENED`.

### 3.3 🎙️ Spoken intro (the natural-language story)
"OK so the model has four objects. `TicketService` is the brain — it owns every ticket, the agent pool, the priority backlog, and the assignment policy. `Ticket` is the interesting one: it's a state machine that guards its own transitions and refuses a second owner, so the service never duplicates those rules. `Agent` is a small entity but not a dumb string — it tracks `active_load`, which the least-loaded policy reads. And `AssignmentStrategy` is the seam — the rule for picking an agent is pluggable. The pieces I deliberately left out are Priority and Status — those are enums, not entities — the Backlog, which is just a heap the service owns, and Comments/Labels, which don't touch the lifecycle so they're out of scope."

---

## 3.5 API / System Interface (the public surface, before the classes)

The public surface of `TicketService` — what external code actually calls. Internal helpers (`_pop_claimable`, `_enqueue`, the lock hooks) are *not* here.

| Method | Signature | Returns | Raises | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `create_ticket` | `(title: str, priority: Priority) -> Ticket` | the new OPEN ticket | `ValidationError` | Reporter files a ticket; it lands in triage |
| `assign_ticket` | `(ticket_id: str, user_id: str) -> Ticket` | the assigned ticket | `TicketNotFoundError`, `AgentNotFoundError`, `AlreadyAssignedError` | Lead assigns one specific ticket to one specific agent (the contended path) |
| `claim_next` | `(user_id: str) -> Ticket` | the claimed ticket | `AgentNotFoundError`, `EmptyBacklogError` | Agent pulls the highest-priority unassigned ticket |
| `auto_assign_next` | `() -> Ticket` | the placed ticket | `EmptyBacklogError`, `NoAgentAvailableError` | System places the top ticket onto a policy-chosen agent |
| `transition` | `(ticket_id: str, status: TicketStatus) -> Ticket` | the updated ticket | `TicketNotFoundError`, `IllegalTransitionError` | Move a ticket along its lifecycle |
| `reopen` | `(ticket_id: str) -> Ticket` | the reopened ticket | `TicketNotFoundError`, `IllegalTransitionError` | Send a RESOLVED/CLOSED ticket back to triage |
| `list_backlog` | `() -> list[Ticket]` | claimable tickets in drain order | — | Show triage, highest-priority-first |

> 🎙️ "The whole system is seven calls: one to file (`create_ticket`), three to get an owner (`assign_ticket`, `claim_next`, `auto_assign_next`), two to move the lifecycle (`transition`, `reopen`), and one read (`list_backlog`). Every method maps to a requirement and back. If you handed me just this table, you'd know exactly what the system does without seeing a single class."

---

## 4. Class Design (per-class derivation, then the diagram)

### 4.1 Per-class derivation (orchestrator first, then the parts)

#### 4.1.1 `TicketService` — the orchestrator

**(a) Role** — The system's public API. External code calls it to file, assign, triage, and move tickets. Everything flows through here; it owns the truth.

**(b) State derivation**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "registry of all tickets, look up by id" | a map: ticket id → Ticket |
| "assign/claim by agent; least-loaded policy" | a map: agent id → Agent |
| "auto-assign via a swappable policy" | the current AssignmentStrategy |
| "triage pops highest-priority next" | a priority heap of pending ticket refs |
| "stable, readable ids + FIFO tie-break" | a monotonic counter |

```
class TicketService:
    - _tickets: Map<string, Ticket>
    - _agents: Map<string, Agent>
    - _strategy: AssignmentStrategy
    - _backlog: Heap<(−priority, seq, ticketId)>
    - _seq: int
```

**State placement trade-off:** A ticket's *physical* state (its status, its owner) lives on the `Ticket` because it describes the ticket's own condition. The *relational* state — which tickets are pending triage (the heap), how the work is distributed (agent loads) — lives in the service because it's a system-managed view across many tickets. The heap holds only ticket *ids*, not status; status is always read back from the entity, so there's one source of truth.

**(c) Operations derivation**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| "file a ticket" | `create_ticket(title, priority) -> Ticket` |
| "assign one ticket to one agent" | `assign_ticket(ticket_id, user_id) -> Ticket` |
| "agent pulls next-highest" | `claim_next(user_id) -> Ticket` |
| "system places via policy" | `auto_assign_next() -> Ticket` |
| "move the lifecycle" | `transition(ticket_id, status) -> Ticket` |
| "send back to triage" | `reopen(ticket_id) -> Ticket` |
| "show triage" | `list_backlog() -> list[Ticket]` |

```
class TicketService:
    - _tickets: Map<string, Ticket>
    - _agents: Map<string, Agent>
    - _strategy: AssignmentStrategy
    - _backlog: Heap<(−priority, seq, ticketId)>
    - _seq: int

    + TicketService(agents, strategy)
    + create_ticket(title, priority) -> Ticket
    + assign_ticket(ticket_id, user_id) -> Ticket | error
    + claim_next(user_id) -> Ticket | error
    + auto_assign_next() -> Ticket | error
    + transition(ticket_id, status) -> Ticket | error
    + reopen(ticket_id) -> Ticket | error
    + list_backlog() -> Ticket[]
    # _lock(ticket)            ← no-op hook; thread-safe subclass overrides
    # _backlog_guard()         ← no-op hook; thread-safe subclass overrides
    # _pop_claimable() -> Ticket
    # _enqueue(ticket)
```

**(d) Design choices worth calling out**
- **Why does `claim_next` reuse `assign_ticket` instead of setting the owner itself?** DRY + safety: the single-owner check lives in one place. If `claim_next` pops a ticket that got assigned a microsecond earlier, `assign_ticket` raises `AlreadyAssignedError` and `claim_next` just pops the next one.
- **Why are the lock hooks on the orchestrator and not the Ticket?** So the clean core has *zero* lock code, and the thread-safe version is one subclass overriding two methods. The diff is ~12 lines.
- **Why hold only ticket ids in the heap?** The heap is for ordering, not truth. Holding ids means a ticket's live status is always read from the registry — no stale duplicate state to keep in sync.

#### 4.1.2 `Ticket` — the lifecycle state machine

**(a) Role** — The heart of the design and the *only* object allowed to change a ticket's status or owner. It guards two rules by construction: legal transitions only, and at most one owner.

**(b) State derivation**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "identity + display" | `id`, `title` |
| "triage ordering" | `priority`, `created_seq` |
| "where in the lifecycle" | `status` |
| "who owns it (if anyone)" | `assignee_id` |

```
class Ticket:
    - id: string
    - title: string
    - priority: Priority
    - created_seq: int
    - status: TicketStatus = OPEN
    - assignee_id: string | null = null
```

**(c) Operations derivation**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| "is it up for grabs in triage?" | `is_claimable() -> bool` |
| "give it an owner, reject a second" | `assign(agent_id) -> void | error` |
| "move status, reject illegal jumps" | `transition(new_status) -> void | error` |
| "reopen: drop owner, back to REOPENED" | `reopen() -> void | error` |

```
class Ticket:
    - id: string
    - title: string
    - priority: Priority
    - created_seq: int
    - status: TicketStatus = OPEN
    - assignee_id: string | null = null

    + Ticket(id, title, priority, created_seq)
    + is_claimable() -> bool
    + assign(agent_id) -> void | AlreadyAssignedError
    + transition(new_status) -> void | IllegalTransitionError
    + reopen() -> void | IllegalTransitionError
```

**(d) Design choices worth calling out**
- **Why does `assign` raise instead of overwrite?** That single guard *is* the no-double-assignment invariant. Whoever check-and-sets first wins; the loser sees `assignee_id` already set.
- **Why route `reopen` through `transition`?** So the legality check still applies — only RESOLVED/CLOSED may reopen; anything else fails with the same clear error.
- **Why no lock on the Ticket?** Locking is the orchestrator's job (per-ticket striping). The entity stays a pure, testable state machine.

#### 4.1.3 `Agent` — assignee with a live workload

**(a) Role** — Represents who work goes to, and carries the one number a fair policy needs: how loaded they are.

**(b) State derivation**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "identity + display" | `id`, `name` |
| "least-loaded policy reads load" | `active_load` |

```
class Agent:
    - id: string
    - name: string
    - active_load: int = 0
```

**(c) Operations derivation**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| (none — load is mutated by the service as a small data holder) | — |

```
class Agent:
    - id: string
    - name: string
    - active_load: int = 0
    + Agent(id, name)
```

**(d) Design choices worth calling out**
- **Why is `Agent` almost behavior-free?** Load is *relational* (the service decides when a ticket joins/leaves an agent), so the service mutates it. Agent stays a small, honest data holder.
- **Why store a count and not a list of tickets?** The policy only needs the *number*. A count is O(1) to read and update; a list would be redundant with the ticket registry.

#### 4.1.4 `AssignmentStrategy` — the pluggable policy (interface)

**(a) Role** — The seam the orchestrator depends on for auto-assignment. The service asks it "who should get this ticket?" and never hardcodes the rule.

**(b) State derivation** — The interface holds no state. (`RoundRobin` keeps a rotation cursor; `LeastLoaded` is stateless.)

```
interface AssignmentStrategy:
    (no state)
```

**(c) Operations derivation**

| Need from requirements | Method on this interface |
|---|---|
| "pick an agent for an auto-assigned ticket" | `pick(agents, ticket) -> Agent | error` |

```
interface AssignmentStrategy:
    + pick(agents: Agent[], ticket: Ticket) -> Agent | NoAgentAvailableError

class RoundRobin implements AssignmentStrategy:
    - _cursor: counter
    + pick(agents, ticket) -> Agent        # agents[next(cursor) % len]

class LeastLoaded implements AssignmentStrategy:
    + pick(agents, ticket) -> Agent        # min(agents, key=active_load)
```

**(d) Design choices worth calling out**
- **Why an interface for two tiny rules?** Because the rule is the thing that changes — skill-based routing, on-call rotation, geography. New rule = new class, zero orchestrator edits (Open/Closed).
- **Why pass `ticket` into `pick` if v1 ignores it?** Future policies (skill-based) need the ticket's attributes; baking it into the contract now avoids a breaking signature change later.

## 5. Design Patterns & Principles

### 5.1 Information Expert (state this first)
I followed Information Expert: each class owns the state and the rule closest to its data. `Ticket` owns its `status` and `assignee_id`, so it enforces legal transitions *and* single-ownership — those rules are intrinsic to a ticket. `Agent` owns its `active_load`. `TicketService` owns the cross-ticket views (registry, backlog, policy) because it's the only thing that sees all tickets at once. The single most important consequence: the orchestrator never writes `ticket.status = X` or `ticket.assignee_id = y` directly — it asks the ticket, and the ticket can refuse. That's why no amount of future orchestrator churn can create an illegal status or a double-owner.

### 5.2 Applied patterns (each earned)

**State (table-driven state machine)**
- **Why it fits here:** the lifecycle is a small, fixed graph of legal edges, and "no illegal jump" is the core safety property. Encoding the edges in one `_LEGAL_TRANSITIONS` map and checking it in `Ticket.transition` makes the rule a single source of truth.
- **Seam:** `Ticket.transition(new_status)` consults `_LEGAL_TRANSITIONS[self.status]`.
- **🎙️ Script:** "The ticket is a state machine. Every legal step is an edge in one map; anything not in the map is rejected. Add a state later? Edit only the map."
- **💬 Layman gloss:** "It's like a board game where each square tells you the only squares you're allowed to move to next — you literally can't make an illegal move."

**Strategy (assignment policy)**
- **Why it fits here:** how the system picks an agent is exactly what the business tweaks. Extracting `AssignmentStrategy` means `auto_assign_next` calls `strategy.pick(...)` and never hardcodes round-robin vs least-loaded.
- **Seam:** `AssignmentStrategy.pick(agents, ticket)` → `RoundRobin` / `LeastLoaded`.
- **🎙️ Script:** "The service doesn't decide who gets the ticket — it asks a policy object. Swapping the policy is a new class, not a core change."
- **💬 Layman gloss:** "Like a dispatcher who follows a rule sheet — change the sheet and the same dispatcher behaves differently, no retraining."

### 5.3 SOLID principles (specific)
- **Single Responsibility:** `Ticket` owns lifecycle + ownership, `AssignmentStrategy` owns agent-selection, `TicketService` owns coordination. Three reasons to change, three classes.
- **Open/Closed:** a new routing rule = a new `AssignmentStrategy` subclass, zero edits to `TicketService`.
- **Liskov:** `ThreadSafeTicketService` is substitutable for `TicketService` — it only strengthens the lock hooks; every public method behaves identically (just atomically).
- **Dependency Inversion:** `TicketService` depends on the `AssignmentStrategy` abstraction, never on `LeastLoaded` directly.

### 5.4 EARN YOUR PATTERNS
Every pattern above is visible in §6/§7 code: the State map is in `models.py` and `Ticket.transition` reads it; the Strategy interface is an `ABC` with two real subclasses that `auto_assign_next` calls; the Template-Method lock hooks are real no-ops overridden by a real subclass that the concurrency test instantiates. I did **not** claim Observer (no notifications in scope), Singleton (the service isn't single-instance-enforced — it's just a Facade over the model), or Factory (we construct objects directly; there's no type-translation step). If we added notifications, I'd wire an Observer at the `transition` seam — but it's an **extension point (not wired in v1)**.

## 5.5 Core Algorithms & Approach

### A. The Key Insight

**Insight:** Make the **Ticket guard both of its own rules** — legal transitions *and* single-ownership — and keep triage a dumb **priority heap** feeding that entity. Then concurrency safety collapses to one tiny atomic step: the check-and-set inside `Ticket.assign`, wrapped by a per-ticket lock.

**Why it works:** "No illegal status jump" and "no double-assignment" are both *intrinsic* properties of a single ticket — they depend only on that ticket's own fields, not on any global view. So they belong on the entity, and the only thing the concurrent world can corrupt is the read-modify-write of `assignee_id`. That's a textbook critical section: small, local, lockable per-ticket. Triage ordering, meanwhile, is pure policy with no per-ticket coupling, so a heap keyed on `(-priority, created_seq)` handles it independently.

**What breaks without it:** The naive version inlines the writes in the orchestrator — `if ticket.assignee_id is None: ticket.assignee_id = user; ticket.status = IN_PROGRESS`. Six months later someone adds a `claim_next` path and forgets to repeat the `None` check, or a second method sets status without consulting the legal map. Now under load two agents both see `None` and both become the owner, or a ticket jumps OPEN → CLOSED. By making `Ticket.assign` and `Ticket.transition` the *only* mutators, both bugs become impossible by construction — every path, present and future, goes through the same guard.

### B. Per-Operation Algorithm (pseudocode + script)

**`create_ticket(title, priority)`**
```
1. if title is empty/blank -> raise ValidationError
2. seq = next counter
3. ticket = Ticket(id="tkt-{seq}", title, priority, created_seq=seq), status OPEN
4. register ticket in _tickets
5. push (-priority, seq, id) onto _backlog heap
6. return ticket
```
> 🎙️ "File-a-ticket is validate-then-register. Step 1 is the boundary guard — an empty title never reaches the registry. Step 5 is the only triage bookkeeping: a heap push, O(log n)."

**`assign_ticket(ticket_id, user_id)` — the pipeline (and the race-prone one)**
```
1. ticket = require ticket_id   (else TicketNotFoundError)
2. agent  = require user_id     (else AgentNotFoundError)
3. LOCK ticket:
4.    ticket.assign(user_id)               # raises AlreadyAssignedError if owned  <-- critical
5.    transition(ticket_id, IN_PROGRESS)   # re-enters the SAME lock (reentrant)
6.    agent.active_load += 1
7. return ticket
```
> 🎙️ "This method has the check-then-act race — step 4 is the critical section. The check (is it owned?) and the act (set the owner) must be one atomic step, so I'll wrap them in a per-ticket lock in §9. Notice step 5 calls the *public* `transition`, which re-takes the same per-ticket lock — that's exactly why the lock has to be reentrant."

**`claim_next(user_id)` — the redemption/drain path**
```
1. require user_id   (else AgentNotFoundError)
2. loop:
3.    LOCK backlog: ticket = pop highest-priority claimable   (else EmptyBacklogError)
4.    try: return assign_ticket(ticket.id, user_id)
5.    except AlreadyAssignedError: continue   # someone grabbed it first; pop the next
```
> 🎙️ "Claim is pop-then-assign. Step 3 hits the heap under the backlog lock; step 4 reuses `assign_ticket` so the ownership guard is identical. The `except` in step 5 is the race handler — if the popped ticket was assigned a microsecond earlier, we just pop the next one instead of failing."

**`auto_assign_next()` — system triage via Strategy**
```
1. loop:
2.    LOCK backlog: ticket = pop highest-priority claimable      (else EmptyBacklogError)
3.                  agent  = strategy.pick(agents, ticket)        (else NoAgentAvailableError)
4.    try: return assign_ticket(ticket.id, agent.id)
5.    except AlreadyAssignedError: continue
```
> 🎙️ "Same shape as claim, but step 3 is where the Strategy fires — `pick` runs under the backlog lock so a stateful policy like round-robin's cursor is safe."

**`transition(ticket_id, status)`**
```
1. ticket = require ticket_id   (else TicketNotFoundError)
2. LOCK ticket:
3.    ticket.transition(status)             # raises IllegalTransitionError if not a legal edge
4.    if status == RESOLVED and ticket has assignee: assignee.active_load -= 1
5. return ticket
```
> 🎙️ "Transition is one guarded write. The legality check lives in the entity (step 3). Step 4 is the only side effect: when work is resolved, the agent's plate clears by one."

**`reopen(ticket_id)`**
```
1. ticket = require ticket_id   (else TicketNotFoundError)
2. LOCK ticket: ticket.reopen()   # transition to REOPENED (legal only from RESOLVED/CLOSED) + clear owner
3. LOCK backlog: push ticket back onto the heap
4. return ticket
```
> 🎙️ "Reopen is two short, *separate* critical sections — never nested — so there's no lock-ordering cycle and no deadlock. First flip the entity, then re-queue it."

**`list_backlog()`**
```
1. claimable = [t for t in _tickets.values() if t.is_claimable()]
2. return claimable sorted by (-priority, created_seq)
```
> 🎙️ "This reads the registry, not the heap — so it never shows stale heap entries. It's the exact order triage would drain."

**(3) DRY RUN — `claim_next("u1")`** with backlog `tkt-2(CRITICAL), tkt-3(HIGH), tkt-4(MEDIUM), tkt-1(LOW)`:

| Step | Action | State change |
|---|---|---|
| 3 | pop highest-priority claimable | heap pops `(-4, 2, "tkt-2")` → `tkt-2` (CRITICAL) |
| 4 | `assign_ticket("tkt-2", "u1")` | enters per-ticket lock for tkt-2 |
| 4a | `tkt-2.assign("u1")` | `tkt-2.assignee_id: None → "u1"` |
| 4b | `transition("tkt-2", IN_PROGRESS)` | `tkt-2.status: OPEN → IN_PROGRESS` (reentrant lock) |
| 4c | load bump | `agent u1.active_load: 0 → 1` |
| return | hand back tkt-2 | caller gets `tkt-2`, now owned + IN_PROGRESS; backlog top is now `tkt-3` |

> *Edge probe: what if a second agent `assign_ticket("tkt-2", "u9")` lands between steps 3 and 4a?* *Without the lock, both read `assignee_id == None` and both win — the race I flagged. With the per-ticket lock, u9 either loses the lock race (then sees `"u1"` and gets `AlreadyAssignedError`) or wins it (then u1 gets the error). Either way exactly one owner. In `claim_next`, the loser's `except` just pops the next ticket.*

### C. Data Structure Choices — with rejected alternatives

| Data Structure | Used For | Why THIS (vs alternative considered) | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| `dict[id → Ticket]` | registry | `assign`/`transition`/`reopen` look up by id constantly; a list would be O(n) per call | O(1) get/set |
| `dict[id → Agent]` | agent pool + load | O(1) agent lookup; `LeastLoaded` reads `active_load` directly | O(1) |
| binary heap `(-priority, seq, id)` | triage backlog | `claim_next` needs the max-priority item; a sorted list re-sorts on every insert, a linear scan is O(n) per claim. Heap is O(log n) push/pop. Lazy deletion skips stale entries. | O(log n) push/pop |
| `dict[id → RLock]` (lock striping) | per-ticket concurrency | one global lock serializes *all* agents even on different tickets; per-ticket locks let different tickets proceed in parallel | O(1) lock fetch |

I deliberately chose **lazy deletion** over eagerly removing assigned tickets from the heap: removing an arbitrary element is O(n). Instead, a ticket grabbed via `assign_ticket` stays as a stale heap entry and `_pop_claimable` skips it (it reads the live `is_claimable()`). I'd switch to an indexed heap / `heapq` with a removed-set + periodic compaction only if reopen/assign churn made stale entries a measurable fraction of the heap (say, >50% at 100K+ live tickets).

### D. Tricky Logic — claim, proof, fix

> **🧠 Claim:** The single-owner guard must be a check-and-set *inside* the per-ticket lock, not split.
> **🔬 Proof:** Two agents call `assign_ticket(tkt-1, …)` at once. Both reach `ticket.assign`; both read `assignee_id is None`; both set themselves. Two owners, one ticket — silent corruption.
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `Ticket.assign` does the `if assignee_id is not None: raise` immediately before the write, and §9's `ThreadSafeTicketService._lock(ticket)` wraps that read-modify-write so only one thread is inside at a time. Proven by `test_concurrent_assign_exactly_one_winner` (50 threads, one winner).

> **🧠 Claim:** The lock must be **reentrant** (`RLock`), not a plain `Lock`.
> **🔬 Proof:** `assign_ticket` holds the ticket lock and calls the public `transition`, which acquires the *same* ticket's lock again on the same thread. A plain `Lock` deadlocks on the second acquire.
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `ThreadSafeTicketService._lock` returns a `threading.RLock()` per ticket id, so the owning thread re-enters freely and only fully releases at the outermost `with`.

> **🧠 Claim:** `claim_next` must tolerate popping a ticket that's already been assigned.
> **🔬 Proof:** Between "pop from heap" and "assign", a concurrent `assign_ticket` can claim that exact ticket. If `claim_next` blindly set the owner it would either double-own or crash.
> **🔬🛠️ Fix in code:** `claim_next` calls `assign_ticket`, catches `AlreadyAssignedError`, and loops to pop the next entry — so a lost race degrades to "get the next ticket", never a failure.

> **🧠 Claim:** Heap ordering must break ties by creation order, or high-volume same-priority tickets starve.
> **🔬 Proof:** With key `(-priority,)` only, Python's heap order among equal priorities is arbitrary; a ticket could sit forever while newer same-priority ones jump ahead.
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** the key is `(-priority, created_seq, id)` — `created_seq` is a monotonic counter, so within a tier it's strict FIFO. `test_backlog_orders_by_priority_then_fifo` covers it.

> **🧠 Claim:** `list_backlog` must read the registry, not the heap.
> **🔬 Proof:** The heap contains stale entries (lazily deleted). Reading it directly would show tickets that are already assigned.
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `list_backlog` filters `_tickets.values()` by `is_claimable()` and sorts — always accurate.

### E. The "what I'd write WRONG first" anti-example

> **❌ Naive version a junior would write:**
> ```
> def assign_ticket(self, ticket_id, user_id):
>     ticket = self._tickets[ticket_id]          # KeyError on bad id
>     if ticket.assignee_id is None:             # check ...
>         ticket.assignee_id = user_id           # ... then act (NOT atomic)
>         ticket.status = TicketStatus.IN_PROGRESS  # direct write bypasses the state machine
>     return ticket
> ```
> **Why it's wrong:** Three failure modes. (1) The check-then-act is non-atomic — two agents both pass the `None` check and both become owner. (2) `ticket.status = …` bypasses the legal-transition guard, so illegal jumps become possible elsewhere. (3) bare `dict[id]` throws an untyped `KeyError` instead of a domain `TicketNotFoundError`. The correct version delegates to `ticket.assign` (entity guards ownership) and the public `transition` (entity guards legality), looks up via `_require_ticket`, and lifts the whole block under a per-ticket reentrant lock in §9.

| 1 | Enums (`Priority`, `TicketStatus`) + legal map | "Let me start with the vocabulary — the priorities, the lifecycle states, and the one map of legal transitions." |
| 2 | `Ticket` (state machine) | "This is the heart — the only class allowed to change a ticket's status or owner, and it refuses anything illegal." |

**1) Enums + the legal-transition map** (`models.py`)

```python copy
class Priority(IntEnum):
    LOW = 1
    MEDIUM = 2
    HIGH = 3
    CRITICAL = 4


class TicketStatus(Enum):
    OPEN = "OPEN"
    IN_PROGRESS = "IN_PROGRESS"
    RESOLVED = "RESOLVED"
    CLOSED = "CLOSED"
    REOPENED = "REOPENED"


# The single source of truth for the lifecycle — every legal step is one edge.
_LEGAL_TRANSITIONS: dict[TicketStatus, frozenset[TicketStatus]] = {
    TicketStatus.OPEN: frozenset({TicketStatus.IN_PROGRESS}),
    TicketStatus.IN_PROGRESS: frozenset({TicketStatus.RESOLVED}),
    TicketStatus.RESOLVED: frozenset({TicketStatus.CLOSED, TicketStatus.REOPENED}),
    TicketStatus.CLOSED: frozenset({TicketStatus.REOPENED}),
    TicketStatus.REOPENED: frozenset({TicketStatus.IN_PROGRESS}),
}
_CLAIMABLE_STATES = frozenset({TicketStatus.OPEN, TicketStatus.REOPENED})
```

**2) The `Ticket` state machine** (`models.py`)

*Role:* the only object allowed to change a ticket's status or owner; it guards both rules.

```python copy
@dataclass
class Ticket:
    id: str
    title: str
    priority: Priority
    created_seq: int                       # monotonic at creation; FIFO tie-break in triage
    status: TicketStatus = TicketStatus.OPEN
    assignee_id: str | None = None

    def is_claimable(self) -> bool:
        # "up for grabs" = nobody owns it AND it's in an open-ish state.
        return self.assignee_id is None and self.status in _CLAIMABLE_STATES

    def assign(self, agent_id: str) -> None:
        # WHY: the no-double-assignment rule lives HERE. First check-and-set wins;
        #      the loser sees assignee_id already set and is rejected.
        if self.assignee_id is not None:
            raise AlreadyAssignedError(f"{self.id} is already assigned to {self.assignee_id}")
        self.assignee_id = agent_id

    def transition(self, new_status: TicketStatus) -> None:
        # WHY: one guard, one truth — if it isn't a legal edge, reject it.
        if new_status not in _LEGAL_TRANSITIONS[self.status]:
            raise IllegalTransitionError(
                f"{self.id}: cannot move {self.status.value} -> {new_status.value}"
            )
        self.status = new_status

    def reopen(self) -> None:
        # WHY: go through transition so legality still applies (only RESOLVED/CLOSED),
        #      then drop the owner so it can be re-triaged.
        self.transition(TicketStatus.REOPENED)
        self.assignee_id = None
```

🎙️ "The ticket owns both invariants — `assign` refuses a second owner, `transition` refuses an illegal jump." ⚠️ "What if two agents call `assign` at once?" → "Inside a per-ticket lock (§9) only one is in the check-and-set at a time; the other sees the owner is set and gets `AlreadyAssignedError`."

**3) The Strategy seam** (`strategies.py`)

```python copy
class AssignmentStrategy(ABC):
    @abstractmethod
    def pick(self, agents: list[Agent], ticket: Ticket) -> Agent:
        raise NotImplementedError


class RoundRobin(AssignmentStrategy):
    def __init__(self) -> None:
        self._cursor = count()

    def pick(self, agents: list[Agent], ticket: Ticket) -> Agent:
        if not agents:
            raise NoAgentAvailableError("no agents registered to take tickets")
        return agents[next(self._cursor) % len(agents)]


class LeastLoaded(AssignmentStrategy):
    def pick(self, agents: list[Agent], ticket: Ticket) -> Agent:
        if not agents:
            raise NoAgentAvailableError("no agents registered to take tickets")
        return min(agents, key=lambda a: a.active_load)
```

        self._agents: dict[str, Agent] = {a.id: a for a in (agents or [])}
        self._strategy: AssignmentStrategy = strategy or RoundRobin()
        self._backlog: list[tuple[int, int, str]] = []     # heap of (-priority, seq, id)
        self._seq = 0

    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self, ticket: Ticket): yield          # no-op hook; §9 subclass overrides
    @contextmanager
    def _backlog_guard(self): yield                 # no-op hook; §9 subclass overrides

    def create_ticket(self, title, priority): ...
    def assign_ticket(self, ticket_id, user_id): ...
    def claim_next(self, user_id): ...
    def auto_assign_next(self): ...

    def reopen(self, ticket_id): ...
