## 4. Class Design (per-class derivation, then the diagram)

### 4.1 Per-class derivation (orchestrator first, then the parts)

#### 4.1.1 `TicketService` — the orchestrator

**(a) Role** — The system's public API. External code calls it to file, assign, triage, and move tickets. Everything flows through here; it owns the truth.

**(b) State derivation**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "registry of all tickets, look up by id" | a map: ticket id → Ticket |
| "assign/claim by agent; least-loaded policy" | a map: agent id → Agent |
| "auto-assign via a swappable policy" | the current AssignmentStrategy |
| "triage pops highest-priority next" | a priority heap of pending ticket refs |
| "stable, readable ids + FIFO tie-break" | a monotonic counter |

```
class TicketService:
    - _tickets: Map<string, Ticket>
    - _agents: Map<string, Agent>
    - _strategy: AssignmentStrategy
    - _backlog: Heap<(−priority, seq, ticketId)>
    - _seq: int
```

**State placement trade-off:** A ticket's *physical* state (its status, its owner) lives on the `Ticket` because it describes the ticket's own condition. The *relational* state — which tickets are pending triage (the heap), how the work is distributed (agent loads) — lives in the service because it's a system-managed view across many tickets. The heap holds only ticket *ids*, not status; status is always read back from the entity, so there's one source of truth.

**(c) Operations derivation**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| "file a ticket" | `create_ticket(title, priority) -> Ticket` |
| "assign one ticket to one agent" | `assign_ticket(ticket_id, user_id) -> Ticket` |
| "agent pulls next-highest" | `claim_next(user_id) -> Ticket` |
| "system places via policy" | `auto_assign_next() -> Ticket` |
| "move the lifecycle" | `transition(ticket_id, status) -> Ticket` |
| "send back to triage" | `reopen(ticket_id) -> Ticket` |
| "show triage" | `list_backlog() -> list[Ticket]` |

```
class TicketService:
    - _tickets: Map<string, Ticket>
    - _agents: Map<string, Agent>
    - _strategy: AssignmentStrategy
    - _backlog: Heap<(−priority, seq, ticketId)>
    - _seq: int

    + TicketService(agents, strategy)
    + create_ticket(title, priority) -> Ticket
    + assign_ticket(ticket_id, user_id) -> Ticket | error
    + claim_next(user_id) -> Ticket | error
    + auto_assign_next() -> Ticket | error
    + transition(ticket_id, status) -> Ticket | error
    + reopen(ticket_id) -> Ticket | error
    + list_backlog() -> Ticket[]
    # _lock(ticket)            ← no-op hook; thread-safe subclass overrides
    # _backlog_guard()         ← no-op hook; thread-safe subclass overrides
    # _pop_claimable() -> Ticket
    # _enqueue(ticket)
```

**(d) Design choices worth calling out**
- **Why does `claim_next` reuse `assign_ticket` instead of setting the owner itself?** DRY + safety: the single-owner check lives in one place. If `claim_next` pops a ticket that got assigned a microsecond earlier, `assign_ticket` raises `AlreadyAssignedError` and `claim_next` just pops the next one.
- **Why are the lock hooks on the orchestrator and not the Ticket?** So the clean core has *zero* lock code, and the thread-safe version is one subclass overriding two methods. The diff is ~12 lines.
- **Why hold only ticket ids in the heap?** The heap is for ordering, not truth. Holding ids means a ticket's live status is always read from the registry — no stale duplicate state to keep in sync.

#### 4.1.2 `Ticket` — the lifecycle state machine

**(a) Role** — The heart of the design and the *only* object allowed to change a ticket's status or owner. It guards two rules by construction: legal transitions only, and at most one owner.

**(b) State derivation**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "identity + display" | `id`, `title` |
| "triage ordering" | `priority`, `created_seq` |
| "where in the lifecycle" | `status` |
| "who owns it (if anyone)" | `assignee_id` |

```
class Ticket:
    - id: string
    - title: string
    - priority: Priority
    - created_seq: int
    - status: TicketStatus = OPEN
    - assignee_id: string | null = null
```

**(c) Operations derivation**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| "is it up for grabs in triage?" | `is_claimable() -> bool` |
| "give it an owner, reject a second" | `assign(agent_id) -> void | error` |
| "move status, reject illegal jumps" | `transition(new_status) -> void | error` |
| "reopen: drop owner, back to REOPENED" | `reopen() -> void | error` |

```
class Ticket:
    - id: string
    - title: string
    - priority: Priority
    - created_seq: int
    - status: TicketStatus = OPEN
    - assignee_id: string | null = null

    + Ticket(id, title, priority, created_seq)
    + is_claimable() -> bool
    + assign(agent_id) -> void | AlreadyAssignedError
    + transition(new_status) -> void | IllegalTransitionError
    + reopen() -> void | IllegalTransitionError
```

**(d) Design choices worth calling out**
- **Why does `assign` raise instead of overwrite?** That single guard *is* the no-double-assignment invariant. Whoever check-and-sets first wins; the loser sees `assignee_id` already set.
- **Why route `reopen` through `transition`?** So the legality check still applies — only RESOLVED/CLOSED may reopen; anything else fails with the same clear error.
- **Why no lock on the Ticket?** Locking is the orchestrator's job (per-ticket striping). The entity stays a pure, testable state machine.

#### 4.1.3 `Agent` — assignee with a live workload

**(a) Role** — Represents who work goes to, and carries the one number a fair policy needs: how loaded they are.

**(b) State derivation**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "identity + display" | `id`, `name` |
| "least-loaded policy reads load" | `active_load` |

```
class Agent:
    - id: string
    - name: string
    - active_load: int = 0
```

**(c) Operations derivation**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| (none — load is mutated by the service as a small data holder) | — |

```
class Agent:
    - id: string
    - name: string
    - active_load: int = 0
    + Agent(id, name)
```

**(d) Design choices worth calling out**
- **Why is `Agent` almost behavior-free?** Load is *relational* (the service decides when a ticket joins/leaves an agent), so the service mutates it. Agent stays a small, honest data holder.
- **Why store a count and not a list of tickets?** The policy only needs the *number*. A count is O(1) to read and update; a list would be redundant with the ticket registry.

#### 4.1.4 `AssignmentStrategy` — the pluggable policy (interface)

**(a) Role** — The seam the orchestrator depends on for auto-assignment. The service asks it "who should get this ticket?" and never hardcodes the rule.

**(b) State derivation** — The interface holds no state. (`RoundRobin` keeps a rotation cursor; `LeastLoaded` is stateless.)

```
interface AssignmentStrategy:
    (no state)
```

**(c) Operations derivation**

| Need from requirements | Method on this interface |
|---|---|
| "pick an agent for an auto-assigned ticket" | `pick(agents, ticket) -> Agent | error` |

```
interface AssignmentStrategy:
    + pick(agents: Agent[], ticket: Ticket) -> Agent | NoAgentAvailableError

class RoundRobin implements AssignmentStrategy:
    - _cursor: counter
    + pick(agents, ticket) -> Agent        # agents[next(cursor) % len]

class LeastLoaded implements AssignmentStrategy:
    + pick(agents, ticket) -> Agent        # min(agents, key=active_load)
```

**(d) Design choices worth calling out**
- **Why an interface for two tiny rules?** Because the rule is the thing that changes — skill-based routing, on-call rotation, geography. New rule = new class, zero orchestrator edits (Open/Closed).
- **Why pass `ticket` into `pick` if v1 ignores it?** Future policies (skill-based) need the ticket's attributes; baking it into the contract now avoids a breaking signature change later.

### 4.2 Final consolidated class diagram

🗣️ **Say, don't draw:** "`Priority` and `TicketStatus` are enums, and the backlog is just a heap field on the service — I won't draw those; I'll focus the diagram on the classes with real logic."

```mermaid

```

**Relationships, one edge at a time:**
- **`ThreadSafeTicketService --|> TicketService` (inheritance):** the thread-safe version *is a* `TicketService` that overrides only the two lock hooks. Clean core unchanged.
- **`RoundRobin ..|> AssignmentStrategy`, `LeastLoaded ..|> AssignmentStrategy` (realization):** both concretes implement the `pick` contract. This is the **extensibility seam**.
- **`TicketService o-- "0..*" Ticket` (aggregation):** the service *holds references to* many tickets in its registry; multiplicity 0..* . Tickets are created by the service but conceptually outlive any single call.
- **`TicketService o-- "0..*" Agent` (aggregation):** the service references the agent pool; it reads/mutates `active_load` but doesn't own the agents' lifetime.
- **`TicketService ..> AssignmentStrategy` (dependency):** the service *uses* the strategy abstraction transiently in `auto_assign_next`; it never depends on a concrete. **This is the seam to point at.**

**Pattern Map**

| Class / Interface | Pattern | One-line reason |
|---|---|---|
| `Ticket` | State (table-driven) | the entity guards its own legal transitions via a transition map |
| `AssignmentStrategy` + concretes | Strategy | `TicketService` depends on the abstraction, not the concrete rule |
| `_backlog` (heap) | Priority Queue | always drains the highest-priority ticket next |
| `TicketService._lock` / `_backlog_guard` | Template Method | no-op hooks in the clean core; thread-safe subclass overrides them |
| `Ticket.assign` single-owner check | Information Expert | the object with the ownership data enforces the ownership rule |

**🎙️ Script: walk the diagram box-by-box.** "I put `TicketService` in the center — every operation flows through it. On the left, `Ticket` — the state machine that owns its status and owner. Below it, `Agent` — a small data holder with a live load. On the right, `AssignmentStrategy` — the Strategy seam; `RoundRobin` and `LeastLoaded` implement it, each a few lines. Underneath the service is `ThreadSafeTicketService` — it *is a* `TicketService` that only swaps the lock hooks. The key seam is `AssignmentStrategy`: the service never knows which policy it's talking to, so new routing rules plug in as new classes with no core change."

## 5. Design Patterns & Principles

### 5.1 Information Expert (state this first)
I followed Information Expert: each class owns the state and the rule closest to its data. `Ticket` owns its `status` and `assignee_id`, so it enforces legal transitions *and* single-ownership — those rules are intrinsic to a ticket. `Agent` owns its `active_load`. `TicketService` owns the cross-ticket views (registry, backlog, policy) because it's the only thing that sees all tickets at once. The single most important consequence: the orchestrator never writes `ticket.status = X` or `ticket.assignee_id = y` directly — it asks the ticket, and the ticket can refuse. That's why no amount of future orchestrator churn can create an illegal status or a double-owner.

### 5.2 Applied patterns (each earned)

**State (table-driven state machine)**
- **Why it fits here:** the lifecycle is a small, fixed graph of legal edges, and "no illegal jump" is the core safety property. Encoding the edges in one `_LEGAL_TRANSITIONS` map and checking it in `Ticket.transition` makes the rule a single source of truth.
- **Seam:** `Ticket.transition(new_status)` consults `_LEGAL_TRANSITIONS[self.status]`.
- **🎙️ Script:** "The ticket is a state machine. Every legal step is an edge in one map; anything not in the map is rejected. Add a state later? Edit only the map."
- **💬 Layman gloss:** "It's like a board game where each square tells you the only squares you're allowed to move to next — you literally can't make an illegal move."

**Strategy (assignment policy)**
- **Why it fits here:** how the system picks an agent is exactly what the business tweaks. Extracting `AssignmentStrategy` means `auto_assign_next` calls `strategy.pick(...)` and never hardcodes round-robin vs least-loaded.
- **Seam:** `AssignmentStrategy.pick(agents, ticket)` → `RoundRobin` / `LeastLoaded`.
- **🎙️ Script:** "The service doesn't decide who gets the ticket — it asks a policy object. Swapping the policy is a new class, not a core change."
- **💬 Layman gloss:** "Like a dispatcher who follows a rule sheet — change the sheet and the same dispatcher behaves differently, no retraining."

### 5.3 SOLID principles (specific)
- **Single Responsibility:** `Ticket` owns lifecycle + ownership, `AssignmentStrategy` owns agent-selection, `TicketService` owns coordination. Three reasons to change, three classes.
- **Open/Closed:** a new routing rule = a new `AssignmentStrategy` subclass, zero edits to `TicketService`.
- **Liskov:** `ThreadSafeTicketService` is substitutable for `TicketService` — it only strengthens the lock hooks; every public method behaves identically (just atomically).
- **Dependency Inversion:** `TicketService` depends on the `AssignmentStrategy` abstraction, never on `LeastLoaded` directly.

### 5.4 EARN YOUR PATTERNS
Every pattern above is visible in §6/§7 code: the State map is in `models.py` and `Ticket.transition` reads it; the Strategy interface is an `ABC` with two real subclasses that `auto_assign_next` calls; the Template-Method lock hooks are real no-ops overridden by a real subclass that the concurrency test instantiates. I did **not** claim Observer (no notifications in scope), Singleton (the service isn't single-instance-enforced — it's just a Facade over the model), or Factory (we construct objects directly; there's no type-translation step). If we added notifications, I'd wire an Observer at the `transition` seam — but it's an **extension point (not wired in v1)**.

## 5.5 Core Algorithms & Approach

### A. The Key Insight

**Insight:** Make the **Ticket guard both of its own rules** — legal transitions *and* single-ownership — and keep triage a dumb **priority heap** feeding that entity. Then concurrency safety collapses to one tiny atomic step: the check-and-set inside `Ticket.assign`, wrapped by a per-ticket lock.

**Why it works:** "No illegal status jump" and "no double-assignment" are both *intrinsic* properties of a single ticket — they depend only on that ticket's own fields, not on any global view. So they belong on the entity, and the only thing the concurrent world can corrupt is the read-modify-write of `assignee_id`. That's a textbook critical section: small, local, lockable per-ticket. Triage ordering, meanwhile, is pure policy with no per-ticket coupling, so a heap keyed on `(-priority, created_seq)` handles it independently.

**What breaks without it:** The naive version inlines the writes in the orchestrator — `if ticket.assignee_id is None: ticket.assignee_id = user; ticket.status = IN_PROGRESS`. Six months later someone adds a `claim_next` path and forgets to repeat the `None` check, or a second method sets status without consulting the legal map. Now under load two agents both see `None` and both become the owner, or a ticket jumps OPEN → CLOSED. By making `Ticket.assign` and `Ticket.transition` the *only* mutators, both bugs become impossible by construction — every path, present and future, goes through the same guard.

### B. Per-Operation Algorithm (pseudocode + script)

**`create_ticket(title, priority)`**
```
1. if title is empty/blank -> raise ValidationError
2. seq = next counter
3. ticket = Ticket(id="tkt-{seq}", title, priority, created_seq=seq), status OPEN
4. register ticket in _tickets
5. push (-priority, seq, id) onto _backlog heap
6. return ticket
```
> 🎙️ "File-a-ticket is validate-then-register. Step 1 is the boundary guard — an empty title never reaches the registry. Step 5 is the only triage bookkeeping: a heap push, O(log n)."

**`assign_ticket(ticket_id, user_id)` — the pipeline (and the race-prone one)**
```
1. ticket = require ticket_id   (else TicketNotFoundError)
2. agent  = require user_id     (else AgentNotFoundError)
3. LOCK ticket:
4.    ticket.assign(user_id)               # raises AlreadyAssignedError if owned  <-- critical
5.    transition(ticket_id, IN_PROGRESS)   # re-enters the SAME lock (reentrant)
6.    agent.active_load += 1
7. return ticket
```
> 🎙️ "This method has the check-then-act race — step 4 is the critical section. The check (is it owned?) and the act (set the owner) must be one atomic step, so I'll wrap them in a per-ticket lock in §9. Notice step 5 calls the *public* `transition`, which re-takes the same per-ticket lock — that's exactly why the lock has to be reentrant."

**`claim_next(user_id)` — the redemption/drain path**
```
1. require user_id   (else AgentNotFoundError)
2. loop:
3.    LOCK backlog: ticket = pop highest-priority claimable   (else EmptyBacklogError)
4.    try: return assign_ticket(ticket.id, user_id)
5.    except AlreadyAssignedError: continue   # someone grabbed it first; pop the next
```
> 🎙️ "Claim is pop-then-assign. Step 3 hits the heap under the backlog lock; step 4 reuses `assign_ticket` so the ownership guard is identical. The `except` in step 5 is the race handler — if the popped ticket was assigned a microsecond earlier, we just pop the next one instead of failing."

**`auto_assign_next()` — system triage via Strategy**
```
1. loop:
2.    LOCK backlog: ticket = pop highest-priority claimable      (else EmptyBacklogError)
3.                  agent  = strategy.pick(agents, ticket)        (else NoAgentAvailableError)
4.    try: return assign_ticket(ticket.id, agent.id)
5.    except AlreadyAssignedError: continue
```
> 🎙️ "Same shape as claim, but step 3 is where the Strategy fires — `pick` runs under the backlog lock so a stateful policy like round-robin's cursor is safe."

**`transition(ticket_id, status)`**
```
1. ticket = require ticket_id   (else TicketNotFoundError)
2. LOCK ticket:
3.    ticket.transition(status)             # raises IllegalTransitionError if not a legal edge
4.    if status == RESOLVED and ticket has assignee: assignee.active_load -= 1
5. return ticket
```
> 🎙️ "Transition is one guarded write. The legality check lives in the entity (step 3). Step 4 is the only side effect: when work is resolved, the agent's plate clears by one."

**`reopen(ticket_id)`**
```
1. ticket = require ticket_id   (else TicketNotFoundError)
2. LOCK ticket: ticket.reopen()   # transition to REOPENED (legal only from RESOLVED/CLOSED) + clear owner
3. LOCK backlog: push ticket back onto the heap
4. return ticket
```
> 🎙️ "Reopen is two short, *separate* critical sections — never nested — so there's no lock-ordering cycle and no deadlock. First flip the entity, then re-queue it."

**`list_backlog()`**
```
1. claimable = [t for t in _tickets.values() if t.is_claimable()]
2. return claimable sorted by (-priority, created_seq)
```
> 🎙️ "This reads the registry, not the heap — so it never shows stale heap entries. It's the exact order triage would drain."

**(3) DRY RUN — `claim_next("u1")`** with backlog `tkt-2(CRITICAL), tkt-3(HIGH), tkt-4(MEDIUM), tkt-1(LOW)`:

| Step | Action | State change |
|---|---|---|
| 3 | pop highest-priority claimable | heap pops `(-4, 2, "tkt-2")` → `tkt-2` (CRITICAL) |
| 4 | `assign_ticket("tkt-2", "u1")` | enters per-ticket lock for tkt-2 |
| 4a | `tkt-2.assign("u1")` | `tkt-2.assignee_id: None → "u1"` |
| 4b | `transition("tkt-2", IN_PROGRESS)` | `tkt-2.status: OPEN → IN_PROGRESS` (reentrant lock) |
| 4c | load bump | `agent u1.active_load: 0 → 1` |
| return | hand back tkt-2 | caller gets `tkt-2`, now owned + IN_PROGRESS; backlog top is now `tkt-3` |

> *Edge probe: what if a second agent `assign_ticket("tkt-2", "u9")` lands between steps 3 and 4a?* *Without the lock, both read `assignee_id == None` and both win — the race I flagged. With the per-ticket lock, u9 either loses the lock race (then sees `"u1"` and gets `AlreadyAssignedError`) or wins it (then u1 gets the error). Either way exactly one owner. In `claim_next`, the loser's `except` just pops the next ticket.*

### C. Data Structure Choices — with rejected alternatives

| Data Structure | Used For | Why THIS (vs alternative considered) | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| `dict[id → Ticket]` | registry | `assign`/`transition`/`reopen` look up by id constantly; a list would be O(n) per call | O(1) get/set |
| `dict[id → Agent]` | agent pool + load | O(1) agent lookup; `LeastLoaded` reads `active_load` directly | O(1) |
| binary heap `(-priority, seq, id)` | triage backlog | `claim_next` needs the max-priority item; a sorted list re-sorts on every insert, a linear scan is O(n) per claim. Heap is O(log n) push/pop. Lazy deletion skips stale entries. | O(log n) push/pop |
| `dict[id → RLock]` (lock striping) | per-ticket concurrency | one global lock serializes *all* agents even on different tickets; per-ticket locks let different tickets proceed in parallel | O(1) lock fetch |

I deliberately chose **lazy deletion** over eagerly removing assigned tickets from the heap: removing an arbitrary element is O(n). Instead, a ticket grabbed via `assign_ticket` stays as a stale heap entry and `_pop_claimable` skips it (it reads the live `is_claimable()`). I'd switch to an indexed heap / `heapq` with a removed-set + periodic compaction only if reopen/assign churn made stale entries a measurable fraction of the heap (say, >50% at 100K+ live tickets).

### D. Tricky Logic — claim, proof, fix

> **🧠 Claim:** The single-owner guard must be a check-and-set *inside* the per-ticket lock, not split.
> **🔬 Proof:** Two agents call `assign_ticket(tkt-1, …)` at once. Both reach `ticket.assign`; both read `assignee_id is None`; both set themselves. Two owners, one ticket — silent corruption.
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `Ticket.assign` does the `if assignee_id is not None: raise` immediately before the write, and §9's `ThreadSafeTicketService._lock(ticket)` wraps that read-modify-write so only one thread is inside at a time. Proven by `test_concurrent_assign_exactly_one_winner` (50 threads, one winner).

> **🧠 Claim:** The lock must be **reentrant** (`RLock`), not a plain `Lock`.
> **🔬 Proof:** `assign_ticket` holds the ticket lock and calls the public `transition`, which acquires the *same* ticket's lock again on the same thread. A plain `Lock` deadlocks on the second acquire.
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `ThreadSafeTicketService._lock` returns a `threading.RLock()` per ticket id, so the owning thread re-enters freely and only fully releases at the outermost `with`.

> **🧠 Claim:** `claim_next` must tolerate popping a ticket that's already been assigned.
> **🔬 Proof:** Between "pop from heap" and "assign", a concurrent `assign_ticket` can claim that exact ticket. If `claim_next` blindly set the owner it would either double-own or crash.
> **🔬🛠️ Fix in code:** `claim_next` calls `assign_ticket`, catches `AlreadyAssignedError`, and loops to pop the next entry — so a lost race degrades to "get the next ticket", never a failure.

> **🧠 Claim:** Heap ordering must break ties by creation order, or high-volume same-priority tickets starve.
> **🔬 Proof:** With key `(-priority,)` only, Python's heap order among equal priorities is arbitrary; a ticket could sit forever while newer same-priority ones jump ahead.
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** the key is `(-priority, created_seq, id)` — `created_seq` is a monotonic counter, so within a tier it's strict FIFO. `test_backlog_orders_by_priority_then_fifo` covers it.

> **🧠 Claim:** `list_backlog` must read the registry, not the heap.
> **🔬 Proof:** The heap contains stale entries (lazily deleted). Reading it directly would show tickets that are already assigned.
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `list_backlog` filters `_tickets.values()` by `is_claimable()` and sorts — always accurate.

### E. The "what I'd write WRONG first" anti-example

> **❌ Naive version a junior would write:**
> ```
> def assign_ticket(self, ticket_id, user_id):
>     ticket = self._tickets[ticket_id]          # KeyError on bad id
>     if ticket.assignee_id is None:             # check ...
>         ticket.assignee_id = user_id           # ... then act (NOT atomic)
>         ticket.status = TicketStatus.IN_PROGRESS  # direct write bypasses the state machine
>     return ticket
> ```
> **Why it's wrong:** Three failure modes. (1) The check-then-act is non-atomic — two agents both pass the `None` check and both become owner. (2) `ticket.status = …` bypasses the legal-transition guard, so illegal jumps become possible elsewhere. (3) bare `dict[id]` throws an untyped `KeyError` instead of a domain `TicketNotFoundError`. The correct version delegates to `ticket.assign` (entity guards ownership) and the public `transition` (entity guards legality), looks up via `_require_ticket`, and lifts the whole block under a per-ticket reentrant lock in §9.

**1) Enums + the legal-transition map** (`models.py`)

```python copy
class Priority(IntEnum):
    LOW = 1
    MEDIUM = 2
    HIGH = 3
    CRITICAL = 4


class TicketStatus(Enum):
    OPEN = "OPEN"
    IN_PROGRESS = "IN_PROGRESS"
    RESOLVED = "RESOLVED"
    CLOSED = "CLOSED"
    REOPENED = "REOPENED"


# The single source of truth for the lifecycle — every legal step is one edge.
_LEGAL_TRANSITIONS: dict[TicketStatus, frozenset[TicketStatus]] = {
    TicketStatus.OPEN: frozenset({TicketStatus.IN_PROGRESS}),
    TicketStatus.IN_PROGRESS: frozenset({TicketStatus.RESOLVED}),
    TicketStatus.RESOLVED: frozenset({TicketStatus.CLOSED, TicketStatus.REOPENED}),
    TicketStatus.CLOSED: frozenset({TicketStatus.REOPENED}),
    TicketStatus.REOPENED: frozenset({TicketStatus.IN_PROGRESS}),
}
_CLAIMABLE_STATES = frozenset({TicketStatus.OPEN, TicketStatus.REOPENED})
```

**2) The `Ticket` state machine** (`models.py`)

*Role:* the only object allowed to change a ticket's status or owner; it guards both rules.

```python copy
@dataclass
class Ticket:
    id: str
    title: str
    priority: Priority
    created_seq: int                       # monotonic at creation; FIFO tie-break in triage
    status: TicketStatus = TicketStatus.OPEN
    assignee_id: str | None = None

    def is_claimable(self) -> bool:
        # "up for grabs" = nobody owns it AND it's in an open-ish state.
        return self.assignee_id is None and self.status in _CLAIMABLE_STATES

    def assign(self, agent_id: str) -> None:
        # WHY: the no-double-assignment rule lives HERE. First check-and-set wins;
        #      the loser sees assignee_id already set and is rejected.
        if self.assignee_id is not None:
            raise AlreadyAssignedError(f"{self.id} is already assigned to {self.assignee_id}")
        self.assignee_id = agent_id

    def transition(self, new_status: TicketStatus) -> None:
        # WHY: one guard, one truth — if it isn't a legal edge, reject it.
        if new_status not in _LEGAL_TRANSITIONS[self.status]:
            raise IllegalTransitionError(
                f"{self.id}: cannot move {self.status.value} -> {new_status.value}"
            )
        self.status = new_status

    def reopen(self) -> None:
        # WHY: go through transition so legality still applies (only RESOLVED/CLOSED),
        #      then drop the owner so it can be re-triaged.
        self.transition(TicketStatus.REOPENED)
        self.assignee_id = None
```

🎙️ "The ticket owns both invariants — `assign` refuses a second owner, `transition` refuses an illegal jump." ⚠️ "What if two agents call `assign` at once?" → "Inside a per-ticket lock (§9) only one is in the check-and-set at a time; the other sees the owner is set and gets `AlreadyAssignedError`."

**3) The Strategy seam** (`strategies.py`)

```python copy
class AssignmentStrategy(ABC):
    @abstractmethod
    def pick(self, agents: list[Agent], ticket: Ticket) -> Agent:
        raise NotImplementedError


class RoundRobin(AssignmentStrategy):
    def __init__(self) -> None:
        self._cursor = count()

    def pick(self, agents: list[Agent], ticket: Ticket) -> Agent:
        if not agents:
            raise NoAgentAvailableError("no agents registered to take tickets")
        return agents[next(self._cursor) % len(agents)]


class LeastLoaded(AssignmentStrategy):
    def pick(self, agents: list[Agent], ticket: Ticket) -> Agent:
        if not agents:
            raise NoAgentAvailableError("no agents registered to take tickets")
        return min(agents, key=lambda a: a.active_load)
```

        self._agents: dict[str, Agent] = {a.id: a for a in (agents or [])}
        self._strategy: AssignmentStrategy = strategy or RoundRobin()
        self._backlog: list[tuple[int, int, str]] = []     # heap of (-priority, seq, id)
        self._seq = 0

    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self, ticket: Ticket): yield          # no-op hook; §9 subclass overrides
    @contextmanager
    def _backlog_guard(self): yield                 # no-op hook; §9 subclass overrides

    def create_ticket(self, title, priority): ...
    def assign_ticket(self, ticket_id, user_id): ...
    def claim_next(self, user_id): ...
    def auto_assign_next(self): ...

**5) `assign_ticket` — the pipeline (6-block format)**

*Role:* give one specific ticket to one specific agent and start work — the one method with the race.

*Core logic:* 1) find the ticket, 2) find the agent, 3) under the per-ticket lock: claim ownership, 4) start work via `transition`, 5) bump the agent's load.

*Edge cases:* unknown ticket id, unknown agent id, ticket already owned (the race loser).

```python copy
def assign_ticket(self, ticket_id: str, user_id: str) -> Ticket:
    ticket = self._require_ticket(ticket_id)
    agent = self._require_agent(user_id)
    with self._lock(ticket):
        ticket.assign(user_id)                              # raises if already owned
        # start work via the public transition — re-acquires the SAME per-ticket
        # lock, which is exactly why the lock must be reentrant.
        self.transition(ticket_id, TicketStatus.IN_PROGRESS)
        self._apply_load_delta(agent, +1)
    return ticket
```

*Reasoning:* The whole method is a thin shell around `ticket.assign`, which is where the single-owner truth lives — the service just supplies the lock and the bookkeeping. Notice we call the *public* `transition` rather than writing `ticket.status` directly: that keeps the legal-transition guard in force and is the reason the lock is reentrant (the same thread takes the ticket lock twice). Notice too that we are *not* validating the status here — `transition` does that. In production we'd also emit an "assigned" event to a notification bus *outside* the lock so a slow webhook never blocks other agents; that Observer seam is noted in §5.4 but not wired in v1.

> 🎙️ Say: "Require, lock, claim, start work, bump load. The check-and-set in `ticket.assign` is the atomic heart; everything else is bookkeeping."
> ⚠️ "Why call `transition` instead of setting status?" → "So the legal-transition guard still runs and there's one mutator for status. It does re-take the ticket lock — that's why it's an `RLock`."

**6) `claim_next` — pop-then-assign**

*Role:* let an agent pull the most important unassigned ticket.

*Core logic:* 1) validate the agent, 2) pop the top claimable from the heap, 3) assign it via `assign_ticket`, 4) on a lost race, pop the next.

*Edge cases:* unknown agent, empty backlog, ticket assigned between pop and assign.

```python copy
def claim_next(self, user_id: str) -> Ticket:
    self._require_agent(user_id)
    while True:
        with self._backlog_guard():
            ticket = self._pop_claimable()                 # raises EmptyBacklogError
        try:
            return self.assign_ticket(ticket.id, user_id)
        except AlreadyAssignedError:
            continue                                       # someone beat us; try the next
```

*Reasoning:* The loop is the elegant part — instead of locking the heap *and* the ticket together (which would invite deadlock), I pop under the backlog lock, release it, then assign under the ticket lock. If the popped ticket was grabbed by a concurrent `assign_ticket`, the `except` catches it and we pop the next entry. Reusing `assign_ticket` means the ownership guard, the lifecycle start, and the load bump are written exactly once.

> 🎙️ Say: "Pop the top, hand it to `assign_ticket`. Lost the race? Pop the next. I never hold the heap lock and a ticket lock at the same time."
> ⚠️ "Could two agents claim the same ticket?" → "No — the pop is under the backlog lock, so each entry comes out once; and even a stale duplicate is caught by `assign_ticket`'s owner check."

### Approach — the `_pop_claimable` helper

This is the one helper with a non-obvious choice: how to skip tickets that left the backlog without being popped (assigned directly via `assign_ticket`).

**Approach 1 — eager removal from the heap (rejected):** on every `assign_ticket`, find and delete that ticket's heap entry.
> **Challenges:** removing an arbitrary element from a binary heap is O(n) (you must search, swap, and re-heapify). Worse, it couples `assign_ticket` to the backlog lock, adding a second lock to the contended hot path and a lock-ordering risk.

**Approach 2 — a parallel "removed" set + indexed heap (rejected for this scale):** keep a `set` of removed ids and a position index for O(log n) deletes.
> **Challenges:** state now lives in two places (heap + set + index) that must stay in sync; forget to update one on reopen and a ticket vanishes from triage. I'd flip to this only past ~100K live tickets with heavy assign/reopen churn.

**Chosen approach — lazy deletion (skip on pop):**
```
pop entries until one is is_claimable(); a stale entry is just discarded.
```
> **Why it wins:** single source of truth — the heap is only a hint; the *ticket* owns its claimable status. No second structure to sync, `assign_ticket` never touches the backlog, and the amortized cost is tiny because stale entries are bounded by assign+reopen volume.

```python copy
def _pop_claimable(self) -> Ticket:
    # Caller holds the backlog guard. Lazy deletion: skip entries no longer claimable.
    while self._backlog:
        _, _, ticket_id = heapq.heappop(self._backlog)
        ticket = self._tickets[ticket_id]
        if ticket.is_claimable():
            return ticket
    raise EmptyBacklogError("no claimable tickets in the triage backlog")
```

### Helper methods

```python copy
def _enqueue(self, ticket: Ticket) -> None:
    # Caller holds the backlog guard. Negate priority so max-priority sorts smallest
    # in Python's min-heap; created_seq breaks ties FIFO.
    heapq.heappush(self._backlog, (-ticket.priority.value, ticket.created_seq, ticket.id))

def _apply_load_delta(self, agent: Agent, delta: int) -> None:
    agent.active_load += delta

def _require_ticket(self, ticket_id: str) -> Ticket:
    ticket = self._tickets.get(ticket_id)
    if ticket is None:
        raise TicketNotFoundError(f"no ticket with id {ticket_id!r}")
    return ticket

def _require_agent(self, user_id: str) -> Agent:
    agent = self._agents.get(user_id)
    if agent is None:
        raise AgentNotFoundError(f"no agent with id {user_id!r}")
    return agent
```

### Verification — full-lifecycle dry runs

**Scenario 1 — Happy path** `create → claim → resolve → close`:
Initial: `_tickets={}`, `_backlog=[]`.

| Step | Call | After |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `create_ticket("login slow", HIGH)` | `tkt-1` OPEN, unassigned; heap `[(-3,1,"tkt-1")]` |
| 2 | `claim_next("u1")` | `tkt-1` IN_PROGRESS, owner u1; `u1.load=1`; heap empty |
| 3 | `transition("tkt-1", RESOLVED)` | `tkt-1` RESOLVED; `u1.load=0` |
| 4 | `transition("tkt-1", CLOSED)` | `tkt-1` CLOSED; load unchanged |

Backlog drained, agent freed. ✓

**Scenario 2 — Illegal jump + reopen:**

| Step | Call | After |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `transition("tkt-1", IN_PROGRESS)` on a fresh OPEN ticket with no owner | legal per map, but in practice we start work via `assign`/`claim`; calling `transition("tkt-1", CLOSED)` on OPEN → **raises `IllegalTransitionError`** (OPEN has no edge to CLOSED) |
| 2 | `reopen("tkt-1")` after it's CLOSED | `tkt-1` REOPENED, owner cleared, re-pushed to heap → claimable again |

**Scenario 3 — Race (plain English):** Without the lock, two agents calling `assign_ticket("tkt-1", …)` both read `assignee_id is None` and both write — two owners. With the per-ticket `RLock`, the read-modify-write is atomic: one thread is inside `assign` at a time, the second sees the owner already set and gets `AlreadyAssignedError`. Exactly one winner, always.

### Complexity Summary

| Operation | Time | Space | Key reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| `create_ticket` | O(log n) | O(1) | heap push on n pending tickets |
| `assign_ticket` | O(1) | O(1) | dict lookup + entity check-and-set |
| `claim_next` | O(k log n) | O(1) | pop until a claimable found; k = stale entries skipped (amortized small) |
| `auto_assign_next` | O(k log n + a) | O(1) | same pop + `strategy.pick` over a agents |
| `transition` / `reopen` | O(1) / O(log n) | O(1) | entity guard; reopen re-pushes the heap |
| `list_backlog` | O(n log n) | O(n) | filter claimable + sort snapshot |

## 8. Key Flow (sequence diagram)

**Which flow and why:** `auto_assign_next` — it's the only flow that touches the heap, the Strategy, *and* the entity's two guards, so it shows how the patterns fire together at runtime.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    actor Triage
    participant S as TicketService
    participant H as Backlog heap
    participant P as AssignmentStrategy
    participant T as Ticket
    Triage->>S: auto_assign_next()
    S->>H: pop highest-priority claimable
    H-->>S: ticket tkt-2
    S->>P: pick(agents, tkt-2)
    P-->>S: agent u2
    S->>T: assign(u2)
    T-->>S: owner set (or AlreadyAssignedError)
    S->>T: transition(IN_PROGRESS)
    T-->>S: status IN_PROGRESS
    S-->>Triage: tkt-2 assigned to u2
```

**🎙️ Walk it arrow by arrow:** "Triage calls `auto_assign_next` on the service. The service doesn't decide order itself — it pops the heap, which hands back `tkt-2`, the highest-priority claimable. Then the service asks the `AssignmentStrategy` to `pick` — *this is where Strategy fires*; `LeastLoaded` returns `u2`. Now the service calls `assign` on the ticket — the ticket's own guard either sets the owner or, if someone beat us, throws `AlreadyAssignedError` (and the service loops to the next ticket). Finally the service calls `transition(IN_PROGRESS)` — the entity's legal-transition guard fires — and hands `tkt-2` back, now owned and in progress."

**Why it shows the design is good:** the service coordinates but implements *no* business logic — ordering is the heap's job, agent choice is the Strategy's job, ownership and lifecycle are the Ticket's job. That's Open/Closed and Information Expert visible in one picture.

## 9. Concurrency, Thread-Safety, Edge Cases & Extensibility

**DEFAULT: describe the solution; write code only if asked.**

🗣️ **Step 1 — Name the exact race:** "In `assign_ticket`, two agents call it on the same ticket. Both run `ticket.assign`, both read `assignee_id is None`, both set themselves. Two owners. That's a check-then-act race — the check and the set aren't one atomic step."

🗣️ **Step 2 — The solution:** "Give each ticket its own lock. Before the check-and-set, acquire that ticket's lock; release after. Now the two agents race for the *lock* — one wins and sets the owner, the other waits, then sees the owner is set and gets `AlreadyAssignedError`."

🗣️ **Step 3 — The trade-off:** "I use one lock *per ticket* (lock striping), not one global lock — so two agents working two different tickets never block each other. Only contention on the *same* ticket serializes, and that's microseconds."

🗣️ **Step 4 — Distributed:** "Across servers an in-process lock is useless — each process has its own memory. I'd push atomicity to the DB: `UPDATE ticket SET assignee=:me, status='IN_PROGRESS' WHERE id=:id AND assignee IS NULL`. Whoever's update touches 0 rows lost the race and gets the typed error — a compare-and-set, no distributed lock needed."

**CONCURRENCY VOCABULARY**

| Term | Plain meaning | Where it appears here |
|---|---|---|
| **Race condition** | two threads touch shared state at once; result depends on timing | two agents in `assign_ticket` on one ticket |
| **Critical section** | the block that must run as one atomic step | the check-and-set in `Ticket.assign` |
| **Check-then-act** | check a condition, then act, but state changed in between | "saw `assignee_id is None`, then set it" |
| **Atomic** | all-or-nothing; no half-done state observable | what the per-ticket lock guarantees |
| **Mutex / lock** | a token only one thread holds at a time | `threading.RLock` per ticket |
| **Reentrant (RLock)** | the same thread can re-acquire a lock it holds | `assign_ticket` holds it, calls `transition`, which re-takes it |
| **Lock striping / granularity** | many small locks (per-resource) vs one global | per-ticket locks in a `dict[id → RLock]` |
| **Pessimistic locking** | grab the lock before touching state | the in-process `RLock` approach |
| **Optimistic locking** | don't lock; write conditionally, retry if you lost | the DB compare-and-set for distributed |
| **Compare-and-set (CAS)** | atomic "update only if value is still X" | `WHERE assignee IS NULL` |
| **Deadlock** | two threads each hold a lock the other needs | avoided: we never hold backlog-lock and ticket-lock together |
| **Starvation** | a thread never makes progress | avoided: locks are short-held; triage is FIFO within a tier |

**CONCURRENCY FOLLOW-UP Q&A**

> ❓ "Why `RLock` and not a plain `Lock`?"
> "Because `assign_ticket` holds the ticket's lock and then calls the public `transition`, which acquires the *same* ticket's lock again on the same thread. A plain `Lock` deadlocks on that second acquire; an `RLock` lets the owning thread re-enter and only fully releases at the outermost `with`."

> ❓ "Why per-ticket locks instead of one global lock?"
> "A global lock serializes the whole system — agents on unrelated tickets would queue behind each other. Per-ticket locks (striping) mean only contention on the *same* ticket serializes. Different tickets proceed fully in parallel."

> ❓ "What's the deadlock risk with two locks (backlog + ticket)?"
> "None, because I never hold both at once. `claim_next` pops under the backlog lock, *releases it*, then assigns under the ticket lock. No thread waits for one lock while holding the other, so there's no ordering cycle."

> ❓ "Pessimistic or optimistic — which and why?"
> "Pessimistic in-process: the critical section is tiny and same-ticket contention is rare, so a short-held lock with no retry loop is simplest. For the distributed case I flip to optimistic — a DB compare-and-set with retry — because you can't hold a Python lock across servers."

> ❓ "Is `list_backlog` safe while assignments happen?"
> "It's a lock-free snapshot read of the registry — it may miss an in-flight assignment by a microsecond, but it never crashes and never shows a ticket as claimable that the entity says isn't. For triage display that staleness is fine; if I needed a consistent view I'd read it under the backlog lock."

❓ **IF ASKED — the thread-safe version.**

**Part A — the no-op hook + subclass.** The clean `TicketService` has two no-op context managers; the subclass overrides only those. `RLock` (reentrant, as above); per-ticket locks (parallelism); and the **double-lock idiom** — lock the dict-of-locks first to fetch-or-create the per-ticket lock (two threads creating the same key at once could corrupt the dict), then hold the per-ticket lock.

```python copy
class ThreadSafeTicketService(TicketService):
    def __init__(self, agents=None, strategy=None) -> None:
        super().__init__(agents, strategy)
        self._locks: dict[str, threading.RLock] = {}
        self._map_lock = threading.Lock()       # guards the dict-of-locks
        self._backlog_lock = threading.Lock()   # guards the heap

    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self, ticket: Ticket):
        with self._map_lock:                                  # 1) lock the map
            lock = self._locks.setdefault(ticket.id, threading.RLock())  # 2) get/create
        with lock:                                            # 3) lock the ticket
            yield

    @contextmanager
    def _backlog_guard(self):
        with self._backlog_lock:
            yield
```

**Part B — the exact modified method.** The core `assign_ticket` is *unchanged* — it already calls `self._lock(ticket)`. The clean version's `_lock` is a no-op; the subclass's `_lock` is a real lock. That's the whole point of the Template-Method hook:

```python copy
# ─── §6 version (clean) — _lock is a no-op, so this is single-threaded-correct only ───
def assign_ticket(self, ticket_id: str, user_id: str) -> Ticket:
    ticket = self._require_ticket(ticket_id)
    agent = self._require_agent(user_id)
    with self._lock(ticket):                       # no-op here
        ticket.assign(user_id)                     # ← race point: check-and-set not atomic
        self.transition(ticket_id, TicketStatus.IN_PROGRESS)
        self._apply_load_delta(agent, +1)
    return ticket

# ─── §9 version (thread-safe) — IDENTICAL body; only _lock changed (in the subclass) ───
def assign_ticket(self, ticket_id: str, user_id: str) -> Ticket:
    ticket = self._require_ticket(ticket_id)       # safe outside the lock: registry read
    agent = self._require_agent(user_id)           # safe outside the lock: pool read
    with self._lock(ticket):                       # now a real per-ticket RLock
        ticket.assign(user_id)                     # check-and-set is now atomic
        self.transition(ticket_id, TicketStatus.IN_PROGRESS)   # reentrant re-acquire
        self._apply_load_delta(agent, +1)
    return ticket
```

🎙️ "The diff is *zero lines in the method* — only the `_lock` hook changed, in a subclass. The two lookups stay outside the lock because they read maps that don't change concurrently. Inside the lock: the check-and-set and the load bump, which must be one atomic step."

**Part C — prove it with a Barrier test** (this is `test_concurrent_assign_exactly_one_winner`, abridged):

```python copy
n = 50
svc = ThreadSafeTicketService(agents=[Agent(f"a{i}", f"A{i}") for i in range(n)])
hot = svc.create_ticket("prod outage", Priority.CRITICAL)
wins, losses, barrier = [], [], threading.Barrier(n)

def attempt(i):
    barrier.wait()                                 # all 50 hit assign at one instant
    try:
        wins.append(svc.assign_ticket(hot.id, f"a{i}").assignee_id)
    except AlreadyAssignedError as e:
        losses.append(e)

ts = [threading.Thread(target=attempt, args=(i,)) for i in range(n)]
for t in ts: t.start()
for t in ts: t.join()
assert len(wins) == 1 and len(losses) == n - 1     # exactly one winner
```

🎙️ "The `Barrier` makes all 50 threads hit `assign_ticket` at the same instant — without it they'd take turns and there'd be no real race. Exactly one wins, 49 get `AlreadyAssignedError`. That's the proof."

**Edge cases handled**

- Empty title → `ValidationError` → `test_create_ticket_empty_title_rejected`.
- Unknown ticket id → `TicketNotFoundError` → `test_assign_unknown_ticket_rejected`, `test_transition_unknown_ticket_rejected`.
- Unknown agent id → `AgentNotFoundError` → `test_assign_unknown_agent_rejected`, `test_claim_next_unknown_agent_rejected`.
- Illegal status jump → `IllegalTransitionError` → `test_illegal_transition_from_open_rejected`.
- Reopen a non-resolved/closed ticket → `IllegalTransitionError` → `test_reopen_open_ticket_rejected`.
- Empty backlog → `EmptyBacklogError` → `test_claim_next_empty_backlog_raises`.
- Empty agent pool on auto-assign → `NoAgentAvailableError` → `test_auto_assign_empty_pool_raises`.
- Same-ticket race → exactly one owner → `test_concurrent_assign_exactly_one_winner`.
- Concurrent drain → no ticket claimed twice → `test_concurrent_claim_no_ticket_claimed_twice`.

**Extensibility — interviewer twists**

*(i) Level calibration:* a junior usually gets none of these; mid-level one or two; senior several with depth. The patterns chosen in §5 are exactly what make these additive.

*(ii) Summary table*

| Want to add | How (one class/method) | What in the core changes |
|---|---|---|
| Skill-based routing | new `SkillBased(AssignmentStrategy)` | nothing — inject it |
| On-call rotation policy | new `OnCallRotation(AssignmentStrategy)` | nothing — inject it |
| New lifecycle state (e.g. `BLOCKED`) | add enum value + edges in `_LEGAL_TRANSITIONS` | one map entry |
| Notify on status change | `Observer` at the `transition` seam | one call inside `transition` |
| SLA escalation (auto-bump priority) | a sweep method reading `created_seq` | one new method, no entity change |

*(iii) Twist walkthroughs*

> **Twist 1 — "Add a `BLOCKED` state a ticket can enter from IN_PROGRESS and leave back to IN_PROGRESS."**
>
> The lifecycle is a fixed map, so this is purely additive.
>
> 🎙️ **What you say:** "I add `BLOCKED` to the `TicketStatus` enum and two edges to `_LEGAL_TRANSITIONS`. No method changes — `transition` already reads the map, so the new state is legal everywhere automatically and illegal jumps to/from it are still rejected."
>
> **The change:**
> ```python
> # TicketStatus: + BLOCKED = "BLOCKED"
> _LEGAL_TRANSITIONS[TicketStatus.IN_PROGRESS] = frozenset({TicketStatus.RESOLVED, TicketStatus.BLOCKED})
> _LEGAL_TRANSITIONS[TicketStatus.BLOCKED] = frozenset({TicketStatus.IN_PROGRESS})
> ```
> **Trade-off:** one map edit; zero risk because the map is the single source of truth. This is the payoff of the table-driven state machine.

> **Twist 2 — "Route tickets by required skill, not round-robin."**
>
> Assignment is already a Strategy, so this is a new class.
>
> 🎙️ **What you say:** "I write `SkillBased(AssignmentStrategy)`; its `pick` filters agents whose skills cover the ticket and falls back to least-loaded among them. I inject it at construction — `TicketService` never changes because it depends on the abstraction."
>
> **The change:**
> ```python
> class SkillBased(AssignmentStrategy):
>     def pick(self, agents, ticket):
>         eligible = [a for a in agents if ticket.priority in a.skills] or agents
>         if not eligible: raise NoAgentAvailableError("no eligible agent")
>         return min(eligible, key=lambda a: a.active_load)
> ```
> **Trade-off:** `Agent` grows a `skills` field; the orchestrator is untouched. Worth it when teams specialize; overkill for a small on-call rotation.

> **Twist 3 — "Auto-escalate priority for tickets sitting too long (SLA)."**
>
> Triage already orders by `(-priority, created_seq)`, so escalation is just re-prioritizing + re-enqueuing.
>
> 🎙️ **What you say:** "I add a `sweep_sla(now)` method a background timer calls each minute: for each claimable ticket older than its SLA, bump its priority and re-`_enqueue` it. The heap naturally floats it up. No entity or assignment change."
>
> **The change:**
> ```python
> def sweep_sla(self, max_age_seq: int) -> None:
>     with self._backlog_guard():
>         for t in self.list_backlog():
>             if self._seq - t.created_seq > max_age_seq and t.priority < Priority.CRITICAL:
>                 t.priority = Priority(t.priority + 1)
>                 self._enqueue(t)
> ```
> **Trade-off:** introduces lazy duplicate heap entries (handled by `_pop_claimable`'s skip) and needs a timer thread; clean for production SLAs, unnecessary for the interview's single-pass scope.

**Hardest follow-up questions**

> ❓ "What's the worst concurrency bug hiding here, and how do you know it's gone?"
> "Double-assignment under a same-ticket race. It's gone because the check-and-set lives in `Ticket.assign` under a per-ticket `RLock`, and `test_concurrent_assign_exactly_one_winner` fires 50 threads through a `Barrier` and asserts exactly one winner, the entity's own `assignee_id` matches that winner, and exactly one agent's load grew."

> ❓ "Multiple app servers — how does assignment stay correct?"
> "In-process locks don't cross servers, so I move the atomic step to the database: `UPDATE ticket SET assignee=:me WHERE id=:id AND assignee IS NULL`. The row count tells the winner from the loser. The triage heap becomes a priority index (or a `SELECT ... ORDER BY priority DESC, created_at LIMIT 1 FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`) so two workers never claim the same row."

> ❓ "10M tickets — does the heap still work?"
> "The live *backlog* is what's in the heap, not all 10M — resolved/closed tickets aren't claimable. If the open backlog itself were huge, I'd shard the heap by priority tier (four heaps) so pops stay O(log n) per tier, and page `list_backlog`. The registry would move to a DB with an index on `(status, priority, created_at)`."

> ❓ "An assignment's downstream side effect (e.g. notify) fails halfway — how do you avoid a half-done assignment?"
> "The in-memory assignment is a single atomic step under the lock, so it's all-or-nothing already. The *notify* is deliberately outside the critical section and best-effort; if it must be reliable I'd write an outbox row in the same DB transaction as the assignment and have a relay deliver it — so the assignment and the 'please notify' fact commit together, and delivery retries independently."

🎙️ **Final 30-second summary:** "The key insight is making the Ticket guard both its own rules — legal transitions and single-ownership — so the orchestrator can never corrupt it. Triage is a priority heap keyed on `(-priority, created_seq)`; agent selection is a pluggable `AssignmentStrategy` (round-robin, least-loaded), so new routing rules are new classes with zero core changes. The one concurrency risk is the same-ticket assignment race; I guard it with a per-ticket reentrant lock via a Template-Method hook — the clean core stays lock-free — and prove it with a 50-thread Barrier test that yields exactly one winner. For multi-server scale I'd push that atomicity into a DB compare-and-set."
